title: Revelation 16
intro: Major violations cluster at vv. 12 (Euphrates) and v. 16 (Armageddon). The plague-by-plague exposition of vv. 2-11 is mostly Rule-compliant. Source: Bohr, _From the Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 41-66, 113-116, 154.

verses.text: And I heard a great voice out of the temple saying to the seven angels, Go your ways, and pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth.

pioneerReadings.html: The commission is given after probation closes. EGW: "When Christ ceases His intercession in the sanctuary, the unmingled wrath threatened against those who worship the beast and his image and receive his mark (Revelation 14:9, 10), will be poured out. The plagues upon Egypt when God was about to deliver Israel were similar in character to those more terrible and extensive judgments which are to fall upon the world just before the final deliverance of God's people" (GC 627.3). Earlier she had seen the moment in vision: "Jesus would not leave the most holy place until every case was decided either for salvation or destruction, and that the wrath of God could not come until Jesus had finished His work in the most holy place, laid off His priestly attire, and clothed Himself with the garments of vengeance. Then Jesus will step out from between the Father and man, and God will keep silence no longer, but pour out His wrath on those who have rejected His truth" (EW 36.1). Agrees with pioneer chronology: probation closes before v.1.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 1-2 together in one quotation block at DAR 641.2; chronology argued at DAR 641.3-643.1
pioneerReadings.html: Post-probation outpouring. Smith argues the plagues are wholly future and fall on the very class warned by the third angel: "this plague clearly reveals at once their chronology; for it is poured out upon those who have the mark of the beast, and who worship his image, — the identical work against which the third angel warns us. This is conclusive proof that these judgments are not poured out till after this angel closes his work" (DAR 641.3). They are "the wine of God's wrath without mixture… Such language cannot be applied to any judgments visited upon the earth while Christ pleads between his Father and our fallen race; hence we must locate them in the future, when probation shall have closed" (DAR 642.3). The temple that issues the commission is the heavenly sanctuary of Rev 11:19: "when the seven angels with the seven golden vials receive their commission, the temple is filled with smoke from the glory of God, and no being can enter into the temple, or sanctuary, till they have fulfilled their work… Christ is then no longer a mediator; mercy, which has long stayed the hand of vengeance, pleads no more" (DAR 643.1).

Miller opens his Lecture 15 on the seven last plagues with verse 1, treating the command as the keynote for the chapter. The vials are poured at the express command of the One who sits in the temple of God, marking these as the closing judicial acts of God upon a defined target — "the earth," meaning the kingdom of the beast or Roman government. Miller then immediately telescopes the whole chapter as a sequential historicist survey: each vial pours upon a definite political-religious phase of the Roman apostasy, beginning at the Reformation and running to the end. This sets the same frame Smith later adopts in DAR, but Miller anchors the first vial to the Protestant exposure of Rome rather than to the modern era. The implied invariant: these plagues are corporate judgments on apostate Christendom, not generic woes on humanity.

The seven last plagues are wholly future, post-second-advent. Litch was the first major Millerite expositor to argue against the historicist past-fulfillment school. "The first question to be settled, in reference to the seven last plagues, is, are they, any or all of them, past, or are they all future?... I have... been constrained to dissent from that view, and consider them what they are emphatically said to be — the seven LAST plagues, in which is FILLED UP the wrath of God" (PREX1 175.1). His four arguments: (1) the plagues follow the Son of man's harvest of Rev 14:14-20; (2) the redeemed sing on the sea of glass before the vials are poured; (3) analogy — Noah safe before flood, Lot before Sodom, Christians before Jerusalem fell; (4) the first vial falls on those who worship the image of the beast, an entity Litch dates to the present century (PREX1 176.3). Litch differs from Smith here only in chronology emphasis — he places the plagues after the second advent rather than between the close of probation and the advent.

The wine of God's wrath = the seven last plagues; chronology fixed by Rev 11:19 and Rev 15:8. Andrews builds the case from chapter 15: "It follows, therefore, that the wine of the wrath of God is the seven last plagues" (TMR 115.1). On post-probation chronology: "The wrath of God, as threatened by the third angel, is poured out in the seven last plagues; for the first plague is inflicted on the very class that the third angel threatens. Compare Revelation 14:9, 10; 16:1, 2. The fact proves that the plagues must be future when the Third Angel's Message is given" (TMR 115.2). And: "wrath without mixture must be wrath with nothing else; that is, wrath without mercy. God has not yet visited the earth with unmixed wrath; nor can he while our great High Priest ministers in the heavenly Sanctuary, and stays the wrath of God by his intercession" (TMR 116.1). Andrews ties the issuing of the vials to the seventh-trumpet opening of the heavenly temple (Rev 11:15-19): "the seven angels do not receive the vials of the wrath of God to pour out upon the earth until the Temple in heaven is opened" (TMR 116.2). Agrees with pioneer.

Haskell opens his Rev 16 chapter with the voice from the temple as the divine command launching the plagues, then immediately ties the first vial to literal famine and crop-failure. He quotes the voice directly — "And I heard a great voice out of the temple saying to the seven angels, Go your ways, and pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth" (SSP 275.2 margin, citing Rev 16:1) — and notes that "ever since the divine fiat uttered on the third day of creation week, the earth has been an obedient servant; and since the creation of man, she has never refused to respond to his call for food... But the first angel poured out his vial upon the earth" (SSP 275.1). The agricultural-disaster reading is anchored in Joel 1:15-20, Hosea 4:3, Habakkuk 3:17, Jeremiah 9:10-11, and Haggai 1:10. Agrees with pioneer that the commission marks the post-probation transition.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 5-6, 67
modernReadings.html: Post-probation outpouring of God's full wrath without mercy, commissioned from the most-holy place of the heavenly sanctuary. Verbatim: "It is not coincidental that God will give the seven angels the plagues in the most holy place of the heavenly sanctuary (Revelation 16:1) where the ark of the covenant is found" (p. 5). On the chronology: "Just before the close of probation God will give every inhabitant of the world the choice either to receive the seal of God or the mark of the beast… When all have made their irrevocable decision, God will release the plague angels" (p. 6). Bohr later catalogues v.1 within his Rev 6-19 "wrath" chain (p. 67): "Revelation 16:1: 'pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth.'" Agrees with pioneer on post-probation chronology.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR)
notes.html: The Lev 16 day-of-atonement framing as the structural backdrop to Rev 15-16 is a structural extension; Smith anchors the chronology via Rev 11:19 / Rev 15:8 / Rev 14:10, not via Lev 16.

verses.text: And the second angel poured out his vial upon the sea; and it became as the blood of a dead man: and every living soul died in the sea.

pioneerReadings.html: The sea becomes as blood — literal, paralleling the Egyptian plagues. EGW quotes the verse and treats it in the cumulative-Egypt frame: "The plagues upon Egypt when God was about to deliver Israel were similar in character to those more terrible and extensive judgments which are to fall upon the world just before the final deliverance of God's people. Says the revelator, in describing those terrific scourges: 'There fell a noisome and grievous sore upon the men which had the mark of the beast, and upon them which worshiped his image.' The sea 'became as the blood of a dead man: and every living soul died in the sea.' And 'the rivers and fountains of waters ... became blood'" (GC 627.3). Agrees with pioneer on literality and the Egyptian-typology.

**Literal sea corrupted as blood of a dead man.** Smith: "A more infectious and deadly substance can scarcely be conceived of than the blood of a dead man; and the thought that the great bodies of water on the earth, which are doubtless meant by the term sea, will be changed to such a state under this plague, presents a fearful picture" (DAR 644.3). Smith treats the plague as literal and notes the surprising-to-English-readers application of "living soul" to "irrational animals, the fish and living creatures of the sea" — using the verse to argue against the natural-immortality reading of Gen 2:7 (DAR 644.3).

Miller reads the second vial as already historical. The sea is "the centre of some great nation… in a restless and turbulent state," and the things living in the sea are persons under its power. "As the blood of a dead man" is not ordinary blood-of-war imagery but "cold, congealed blood… a massacre in cool blood, without any resistance on the part of those murdered." Miller fixes the fulfillment in France, A.D. 1572, the St. Bartholomew’s eve massacre of the Huguenots, "when 50,000 were slain in one night, and the streets ran blood, as Sully tells us, in some places ankle deep, in the city of Paris." He notes the king himself "stood in his balcony, and shot down his naked and defenseless subjects." The vial falls on France because France was "the stoutest of the Papal horns — the chief instrument in establishing, building up, and supporting that cruel, murderous power of Papacy." This is a past-fulfilled, France-specific reading, structurally identical to Smith’s later DAR treatment but two decades earlier and with the same historical anchor.

Literal sea-judgment paralleling the first Egyptian plague (Exod 7:17-21). Litch sets the controlling principle for the whole chapter: "The vials, I must regard, as producing the same literal effects ascribed to them in the 16th chapter of Revelation. The plagues are almost identical with those which came on Egypt when God delivered his people, and I can see no reason why these will not be as literal as those" (PREX1 177.1). Quoting the verse, he then cross-references Exod 7:17-21 at length as illustration (PREX1 178.1-2). Agrees with pioneer.

Andrews quotes vv. 3-7 in one block and reads them through the Egyptian-plague type: "As an illustration of these plagues, read Exodus 7:17-21" (TMR 118.1). On the moral logic of v.3: "The third vial is a retribution for the blood of the saints. As the blood of all the righteous that had been slain upon the earth came upon that generation which rejected Christ at his first advent (Matthew 23:34-36), so also may it be now. It should be remembered that these fearful judgments are inflicted in consequence of the acts described in the conclusion of Revelation 13" (TMR 118.2). Treats the sea-plague as literal and post-probation. Agrees with pioneer.

Literal sea-judgment with a scientific gloss. Haskell: "The vial of the second angel was poured upon the sea, and the creatures of the sea died, for what was once life became poison. There is but a step between life and death. A change of a few degrees in temperature, would kill all life, both animal and vegetable; deprive an animal of the life-giving oxygen, and in a few moments, life is extinct" (SSP 277.2). He also draws the Goshen parallel — God divides between His sealed people and the worshipers of the beast as He did between Israel and Egypt: "The deliverance of Israel from the land of Egypt, and their guidance through the wilderness, is a type of God's care for His sealed ones during the year when the plagues are falling" (SSP 277.3). Agrees with pioneer.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 25
modernReadings.html: Literal sea-turned-to-blood as part of God's vindication of the martyrs (Bohr links the second and third plagues to the cry of the fifth-seal martyrs and the death decree against the 144,000). Verbatim from the EGW block he quotes: "The sea 'became as the blood of a dead man: and every living soul died in the sea'" (p. 25, citing GC 627). On the moral logic: "The second and third plagues — the seas and fountains of waters turned into blood — are not only God's response to the pleas of the martyrs of the past but also His answer to the death decree against the 144,000" (p. 9, paraphrase of pp. 9-10). Agrees with pioneer on literality.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at this verse)
notes.html: The cross-link to Exod 7:17-21 as the first-Egyptian-plague parallel is a structural cross-reference; Smith cites Exod 7:17-21 explicitly under the third plague (DAR 645.2), not the second.

verses.text: And the third angel poured out his vial upon the rivers and fountains of waters; and they became blood.

pioneerReadings.html: Rivers and fountains turned to blood as judgment-in-kind for the blood of saints. EGW quotes the verse in the same GC 627.3 block: "And 'the rivers and fountains of waters ... became blood.' Terrible as these inflictions are, God's justice stands fully vindicated." Agrees with pioneer on literality and on the moral logic that the same generation incurs the guilt of all martyr-blood ("By condemning the people of God to death, they have as truly incurred the guilt of their blood as if it had been shed by their hands. In like manner Christ declared the Jews of His time guilty of all the blood of holy men which had been shed since the days of Abel" — GC 627.3).

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 4-7 together in one quotation block at DAR 644.4; third-plague comment at DAR 644.5-645.2
pioneerReadings.html: **Literal rivers and fountains turned to blood.** Smith: "Such is the description of the terrible retribution for the 'blood of saints' shed by violent hands, which will be given to those who have done, or wish to do, such deeds. And though the horrors of that hour when the fountains and rivers of water shall be like blood, cannot now be realized, the justice of God will stand vindicated, and his judgments approved" (DAR 644.5). Smith ties duration to the Egyptian parallel: "It would seem that none of the human family could long survive a continuance of a plague so terrible as this. It must therefore be limited in its duration, as was the similar one on Egypt. Exodus 7:17-21, 25" (DAR 645.2).

Miller reads the third vial as destructive war on the nations and states surrounding the previous "sea," i.e. the Papal kingdoms. "Rivers and fountains of water" = "the nations and states who live around the centre, or sea, as it was called in the preceding vial"; "blood" = "destructive war." He dates the vial about A.D. 1630, lasting nearly fifty years, and enumerates fulfillments: thirty-year wars in Spain and Portugal; the French civil wars; the English civil wars from Charles I in 1642 to George I in 1714; and especially the Thirty Years’ War in Germany between the Evangelical League and the Catholic League (citing Guthrie, vol. 1, p. 443), with Gustavus Adolphus killed at Lützen in 1632 and the war ending at the Peace of Münster in 1648. The lex talionis is explicit: the vial answers for Papal blood-guilt against the Protestants. This is broader than Smith’s narrower French-Revolution framing for vial three; Miller spreads the third vial across the whole seventeenth-century continental bloodletting against Protestant nations.

Litch quotes vv. 3-7 in one block (PREX1 178.1) and applies Exod 7:17-21 to both the second and third vials. "This judgment will be a retribution for 'the blood of the saints'" (PREX1 178.2). Treats the rivers and fountains as literal. Agrees with pioneer.

Andrews treats the third vial as retribution-in-kind: "The third vial is a retribution for the blood of the saints. As the blood of all the righteous that had been slain upon the earth came upon that generation which rejected Christ at his first advent (Matthew 23:34-36), so also may it be now" (TMR 118.2). Anchored in Exod 7:17-21. Agrees with pioneer.

Haskell reads the third plague as a withdrawal of the life-giving Spirit from the rivers and fountains: "The third angel withdraws the life-giving spirit from the rivers and the fountains of water, and they become blood. Since the days of creation, God has, by the flowing streams and the wells of water, typified salvation, which is full and free. As a teacher on earth, Christ used the waters of Jacob's well, to illustrate the life of the Spirit, which wells up into everlasting life... Those who have exchanged life for death, will see the rivers turned to blood — a type of the blood of Christ, which they have spurned; and the lives of saints which they have lightly regarded" (SSP 278.2). Agrees with pioneer on literality; adds an inverted-water-of-life typology not in DAR.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 9-10, 25
modernReadings.html: Literal rivers and fountains turned to blood as judgment-in-kind for the death decree against the 144,000. Bohr quotes the verse directly (p. 9: "the rivers and fountains of waters… became blood") and reads it as God avenging shed and intended-shed blood. Verbatim on the moral logic: "the second and third plagues — the seas and fountains of waters turned into blood — are not only God's response to the pleas of the martyrs of the past but also His answer to the death decree against the 144,000" (paraphrase of pp. 9-10). Agrees with pioneer.

verses.text: And I heard the angel of the waters say, Thou art righteous, O Lord, which art, and wast, and shalt be, because thou hast judged thus.

pioneerReadings.html: The angelic vindication is quoted directly: "The angel of God declares: 'Thou art righteous, O Lord, ... because Thou hast judged thus. For they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and Thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy.' Revelation 16:2-6" (GC 627.3). The verse stands for the heavenly verdict that the post-probation wrath is just. Agrees with pioneer.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 4-7 within the block at DAR 644.4; angelic-vindication comment at DAR 644.5
pioneerReadings.html: Heavenly vindication of the third plague. Smith reports the angelic response directly: "Even the angels are heard exclaiming, Thou art righteous, O Lord, because thou hast judged thus; for they have shed the blood of saints and prophets. Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments" (DAR 644.5).

Miller treats the angel of the waters as the voice of theodicy explaining the third vial. "They have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and thou hast given them blood to drink, for they are worthy" is, for Miller, the explicit ground of the preceding judgment: God’s vials are not arbitrary outpourings but measured retribution proportioned to Papal blood-guilt. He treats the verse as a confirmation back-clip — the divine commentary that proves his identification of the third vial with the European bloodbath of 1630-1680: "In these verses we have the reasons given, why they were visited with this scourge of war, because, in the preceding vial, they had shed the blood of saints; which proves that the exposition of that vial, which I have already given, is correct." This is structurally identical to Smith’s later reading: a corporate, retributive, past-fulfilled vial culminating in a vindication of divine justice.

Litch quotes vv. 3-7 within his block (PREX1 178.1) without separating v.5 from the wider angel-of-the-waters vindication chorus; his exposition focuses on the literal sea/rivers (vv.3-4) and the retribution-for-blood theme (v.6) rather than on v.5's address to God specifically. Treats the angelic speech as the heavenly verdict on the third plague. Agrees with pioneer.

Andrews quotes the angel-of-the-waters declaration as part of his block-quote of vv. 3-7 (TMR 117.3) — "And I heard the angel of the waters say, Thou art righteous, O Lord, which art, and wast, and shalt be, because thou hast judged thus" — and reads it as the heavenly verdict that the third plague is just retribution for shed blood (TMR 118.2). Agrees with pioneer.

Haskell reads the angel-of-the-waters speech as heaven's continual presence with earth through the time of trouble: "Heaven is bending near the earth, even through its time of trouble; and angels, having watched the workings of evil, uphold the purpose of God, and pronounce His judgments true and righteous" (SSP 279.1). He cites Rev 14:18 (angel with power over fire) and Rev 16:5 (angel with power over water) in his angelology summary at SSP 290.2 as evidence that specific angels are stationed over specific elements. Agrees with pioneer.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 7
modernReadings.html: Heavenly vindication; Bohr quotes EGW's _Testimonies to Ministers_ sketch of the angel-of-the-waters declaration verbatim: "And I heard the angel of the waters say, 'Thou art righteous, O Lord, which art, and wast, and shalt be, because Thou hast judged thus.' These heavenly beings, in executing the mandate of God, ask no questions, but do as they are bid" (p. 7, quoting TM 432). Agrees with pioneer.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR)
notes.html: The threefold "art, wast, shalt be" cross-link to Rev 1:4, 8; 4:8 as identifying the eternal covenant LORD is a structural extension; Smith does not develop the formula in this paragraph.

verses.text: For they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy.

pioneerReadings.html: Judgment-in-kind anchored in motive-guilt, with the Matt 23 / Abel chain explicit. EGW: "By condemning the people of God to death, they have as truly incurred the guilt of their blood as if it had been shed by their hands. In like manner Christ declared the Jews of His time guilty of all the blood of holy men which had been shed since the days of Abel; for they possessed the same spirit and were seeking to do the same work with these murderers of the prophets" (GC 627.3). EGW thus supplies the explicit Abel-onward sweep that the editor's note flags as not in Smith's exposition at this verse.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 4-7 within the block at DAR 644.4; comment on motive-guilt at DAR 645.1
pioneerReadings.html: **Judgment in kind** — they have blood to drink. Smith addresses the obvious objection (the last generation of saints is not actually slain) by anchoring on motive: "It may be asked how the last generation of the wicked can be said to have shed the blood of saints and prophets, since the last generation of saints are not to be slain. A reference to Matthew 23:34, 35; 1 John 3:15, will explain. These scriptures show that guilt attaches to motive no less than to action; and no generation ever formed a more determined purpose to devote the saints to indiscriminate slaughter than the present generation will, not far in the future. (See chapter 12:17; 13:15.) In motive and purpose, they do shed the blood of saints and prophets, and are every whit as guilty as though they were able to carry out their wicked intentions" (DAR 645.1).

For Miller, verse 6 is the explicit lex talionis statement that fixes the meaning of the third vial. The Papal powers "have shed the blood of saints and prophets" — referring to centuries of persecution of Protestants, Waldenses, Huguenots, and dissenters — and "thou hast given them blood to drink, for they are worthy." Miller insists this is not metaphorical but a real historical accounting: the seventeenth-century wars of religion are the cup of blood given to drink to the very nations that had previously shed the blood of the saints. He emphasizes that this proves his preceding exposition correct: only a vial poured upon the formerly-persecuting Papal kingdoms can answer the language of "blood to drink." This reading converges with Smith’s lex-talionis framing in DAR but expands the historical fulfillment from the French Revolution back into the broader continental wars.

Litch treats vv.3-7 as a single block and quotes v.6 to clinch the typological link to Exodus 7:17-21 (Nile-to-blood). He writes that the second and third vials are illustrated by the Egyptian plague — Aaron stretches his rod and "all the waters that were in the river were turned to blood." "This judgment will be a retribution for ‘the blood of the saints.’" The connection is exegetically tight: as Pharaoh’s Nile-massacre of Hebrew infants was answered by water-to-blood, so the persecuting kingdoms’ massacre of saints and prophets is answered by water-to-blood under vials two and three. Litch reads the verse as future and literal (he writes in 1842 expecting the vials to be poured imminently), aligning the antitype precisely with the type. This is structurally identical to Miller and Smith on the lex-talionis logic, but Litch makes the Exodus-typology explicit where they leave it implicit, and treats the bloodletting as literal future rather than already-fulfilled European wars (Miller’s reading).

Andrews makes the Matthew 23:34-36 chain explicit: "The third vial is a retribution for the blood of the saints. As the blood of all the righteous that had been slain upon the earth came upon that generation which rejected Christ at his first advent (Matthew 23:34-36), so also may it be now" (TMR 118.2). The post-probation generation inherits the cumulative guilt for all martyr-blood. Agrees with pioneer's motive-guilt logic; supplies the same Matt 23 anchor.

Haskell reads v.6 as the divine reckoning for spurned blood: "Those who have exchanged life for death, will see the rivers turned to blood — a type of the blood of Christ, which they have spurned; and the lives of saints which they have lightly regarded" (SSP 278.2). The marginal scripture chain pairs Rev 16:6 with Heb 10:29 (treading underfoot the Son of God) and Mal 3:5 (the Lord's witness against those who oppress). Agrees with pioneer on the moral-equivalence reading.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 9-10, 25, 30
modernReadings.html: Judgment-in-kind; Bohr roots the verdict in motive, citing the EGW + Matt 23 chain directly. Verbatim: "By condemning the people of God to death, they have as truly incurred the guilt of their blood as if it had been shed by their hands. In like manner Christ declared the Jews of His time guilty of all the blood of holy men which had been shed since the days of Abel" (p. 9, quoting GC 627). Bohr ties the verse explicitly to the future death decree against the 144,000: "Revelation 16:5-7 clearly indicates that the wicked will ratify the death decree because of the devastating effects of the first two plagues" (p. 30). Agrees with pioneer on motive-guilt logic.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at this verse)
notes.html: The "lex talionis" frame and the Gen 9:6 / Matt 23:35 cross-link as a sweep "from Abel onward through the 1260 years" is a structural extension; Smith's cross-references here are Matt 23:34-35; 1 John 3:15; Rev 12:17; Rev 13:15, not Gen 9:6 or a 1260-year arc.

verses.text: And I heard another out of the altar say, Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments.

pioneerReadings.html: Ellen White treats the altar-voice as part of a single vindication block (Rev 16:2-6), and she explicitly cites the lex talionis logic of v.7: "By condemning the people of God to death, they have as truly incurred the guilt of their blood as if it had been shed by their hands. In like manner Christ declared the Jews of His time guilty of all the blood of holy men which had been shed since the days of Abel; for they possessed the same spirit and were seeking to do the same work with these murderers of the prophets." The verse therefore stands as the divine ratification of all the preceding vials: heaven itself, including the altar where the souls of the slain have pleaded "How long?", declares the judgments true and righteous. For EGW, the vial-period is the answer to Rev 6:10 — God’s "strange act" (Isa 28:21) finally executed, "wrath unmingled with mercy," after probation closes. This converges with Miller (MWV2 222) and Smith (DAR) on the corporate-guilt principle and grounds the entire vial-cycle as forensically just rather than capricious.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 4-7 within the block at DAR 644.4; vindication chorus at DAR 644.5
pioneerReadings.html: The altar voice joins the angelic vindication. Smith treats vv. 5-7 as a continuous response chorus: the angel of the waters speaks first (v. 5-6), and "another out of the altar" (v. 7) confirms — "Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments" (quoted at DAR 644.5).

Miller treats the altar voice as a second witness ratifying the divine verdict of vial three. "And I heard another out of the altar say, Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments" — the corroboration shows that heaven itself, including the souls under the altar (Rev 6:9-11), declares the historical bloodletting of the seventeenth-century Papal wars to be just. Miller folds this directly into his exposition of vials 3-4 without granting verse 7 a separate paragraph: it is the closing seal on the doctrine of measured retribution. The implication is that the saints whose blood was shed are vindicated under vial three, and the Papal kingdoms’ judgment is therefore not capricious but a long-awaited answer to the cry "How long?" This converges with Smith’s reading and with Andrews’ TMR exposition.

Litch quotes v.7 as part of his block on vv. 3-7 (PREX1 178.1) but does not separate it out for exposition; he treats the altar-voice as confirming the angel-of-the-waters declaration that the third plague is just retribution for shed blood. Agrees with pioneer.

Andrews quotes the altar-voice chorus within his vv. 3-7 block (TMR 117.3): "And I heard another out of the altar say, Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments." Treats it as the confirming heavenly verdict on the third plague's justice. Agrees with pioneer.

Haskell folds v.7 into the angelic-vindication chorus of vv. 5-6: "angels, having watched the workings of evil, uphold the purpose of God, and pronounce His judgments true and righteous" (SSP 279.1). The altar-voice is heaven's amen to the angel-of-the-waters' verdict. Agrees with pioneer.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 9, 25
modernReadings.html: The altar voice chorus confirms the angel of the waters; Bohr does explicitly make the Rev 6:9-11 / Rev 16:5-7 link Smith leaves implicit. Verbatim: "The expression in the song 'just and true are your ways' brings to mind the pleas of the martyrs that are under the altar in the fifth seal (Revelation 6:9-11)" (p. 9). Bohr then quotes the verses in his block quote of GC 627: "Thou art righteous, O Lord… For they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and Thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy" (p. 25). Agrees with pioneer.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR)
notes.html: The cross-link to the souls-under-the-altar cry of Rev 6:9-11 as the cry now being _answered_ under the third plague is a structural extension; Smith does not make that Rev-6/Rev-16 linkage in this paragraph.

verses.text: And the fourth angel poured out his vial upon the sun; and power was given unto him to scorch men with fire.

pioneerReadings.html: Literal scorching by the sun, anchored by Joel 1 and Amos 8. EGW: "In the plague that follows, power is given to the sun 'to scorch men with fire. And men were scorched with great heat.' Verses 8, 9. The prophets thus describe the condition of the earth at this fearful time: 'The land mourneth; ... because the harvest of the field is perished.... All the trees of the field are withered: because joy is withered away from the sons of men.' 'The seed is rotten under their clods, the garners are laid desolate.... How do the beasts groan! the herds of cattle are perplexed, because they have no pasture.... The rivers of waters are dried up, and the fire hath devoured the pastures of the wilderness.' 'The songs of the temple shall be howlings in that day, saith the Lord God: there shall be many dead bodies in every place; they shall cast them forth with silence.' Joel 1:10-12, 17-20; Amos 8:3" (GC 628.1). Adds the limiting principle: "These plagues are not universal, or the inhabitants of the earth would be wholly cut off" (GC 628.2). Agrees with pioneer; same Joel anchor.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 8-9 together in one quotation block at DAR 645.3; comment at DAR 645.4
pioneerReadings.html: **Literal scorching from the sun, compounding the previous plagues.** Smith stresses the cumulative character of the plagues: "It is worthy of notice that every succeeding plague tends to augment the calamity of the previous ones and highten the anguish of the guilty sufferers. We have now a noisome and grievous sore preying upon men, inflaming their blood, and pouring its feverish influence through their veins. In addition to this, they have only blood to allay their burning thirst; and, as if to crown all, power is given unto the sun, and he pours upon them a flood of liquid fire, and they are scorched with great heat" (DAR 645.4). Smith notes that "under this plague occur, beyond question, the scenes of drought and famine so graphically described by Joel 1:14-20… 'the rivers of waters are dried up'" (DAR 647.4).

Miller reads the fourth vial as a vial poured on the gospel itself, weaponized against the Papal kingdoms. "The sun is the great source of light and heat, and, in prophetic language, is an emblem of the gospel," citing Psalm 19:4-10. "To scorch men with fire signifies to make men angry; great heat, uncommonly angry, vengeful, malicious." Miller dates the vial to the eighteenth century, fulfilled in the rise of organized deism and infidelity under Frederick of Prussia and his French confederates — Voltaire, the Encyclopedists, and "all the wits, men of genius and learning, as they boasted, of all Europe and America" who "in their secret assemblies, or clubs… went so far as to calculate about what length of time it would take for them to destroy and exterminate the religion of the Galilean and his twelve fishermen." This is a striking divergence from Smith’s DAR reading, which puts the fourth vial in the modern era (heat-waves, droughts). Miller’s sun = gospel; Smith’s sun = literal solar heat.

Litch quotes vv. 8-11 in a block (PREX1 179.1) and comments tersely: "The scorching of fire in the midst of their trouble, will be terrible in the extreme." The plague is literal and adds to the agony of the preceding vials. Agrees with pioneer.

Andrews quotes vv. 8-11 and comments: "The scorching of the fire in the midst of their trouble will be terrible in the extreme" (TMR 119.1) — using the same phrasing later echoed in Smith. The fourth plague is literal scorching that compounds the preceding judgments. Agrees with pioneer.

Haskell reads the fourth plague as the withdrawal of God's Spirit from the sun: "The sun, which has shone alike upon the just and the unjust, which, in its very beams, is a reflection of the smile of God, becomes, when His Spirit is withdrawn, a heat which scorches men as with fire. God, whose countenance is life to those who are in harmony with Him, is a consuming fire to His enemies" (SSP 279.1). On the protection of the sealed: "In the wilderness a cloud overshadowed the camp by day. God was as a 'shadow of a great rock in a weary land.' 'He that dwelleth in the secret place of the most High shall abide under the shadow of the Almighty.' But those without a shelter, who suffer from the intense heat, which withers every living thing, and prostrates man and beast, blaspheme God and repent not" (SSP 280, on vv. 8-9). Agrees with pioneer on literality.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 25-26
modernReadings.html: Literal scorching; Bohr quotes GC's block citing Joel 1 and Amos 8 as the prophetic backdrop. Verbatim: "In the plague that follows, power is given to the sun 'to scorch men with fire. And men were scorched with great heat.' Verses 8, 9" (p. 25). On the chronological signal: "The land mourneth… The rivers of water are dried up, and the fire hath devoured the pastures of the wilderness" (p. 25, GC 627-628 quoting Joel 1:10-12, 17-20; Amos 8:3). Agrees with pioneer; same Joel anchor.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at this verse)
notes.html: Cross-links to Isa 24:6 and Mal 4:1 are structural extensions; Smith's explicit cross-reference under the fourth plague is Joel 1:14-20 (cited later at DAR 647.4).

verses.text: And men were scorched with great heat, and blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues: and they repented not to give him glory.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads v.9 as the impenitence-clause of the fourth vial in continuity with v.8. After describing the scorching as a literal future drought of unprecedented severity — invoking Joel 1:10-20 and Amos 8:3 to describe a famine in which "the land mourneth… the harvest of the field is perished… the rivers of water are dried up" — she insists on the categorical novelty of these final judgments: "These plagues are not universal, or the inhabitants of the earth would be wholly cut off. Yet they will be the most awful scourges that have ever been known to mortals. All the judgments upon men, prior to the close of probation, have been mingled with mercy. The pleading blood of Christ has shielded the sinner from receiving the full measure of his guilt; but in the final judgment, wrath is poured out unmixed with mercy." The wicked blaspheme rather than repent because probation has closed and the Spirit of God is withdrawn — they cannot now turn. This converges with Miller and Smith on the post-probation hardening and grounds it theologically in the cessation of Christ’s intercession.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 8-9 within the block at DAR 645.3; comment at DAR 645.4
pioneerReadings.html: The wicked respond with blasphemy, not repentance. Smith: "Here, as the record runs, their woe first seeks utterance in fearful blasphemy" (DAR 645.4).

For Miller, verse 9 is the closing seal on the fourth vial’s deistic fulfillment: "they repented not to give him the glory." He observes plainly that "it is not known that any of these principal deistical writers were converted to the religion of Jesus Christ before their death, to give God the glory. Yet I think we have some account of many of them dying in horror, at the frightful view of the future, on consequence of their blasphemous lives against the majesty of the King of kings. Therefore, in the history of the Deists in the eighteenth century, we have the history of the plague of the fourth vial." This is again a sharp divergence from Smith: where DAR reads "blasphemed the name of God" as the modern era’s climatic suffering greeted with profanity rather than repentance, Miller reads it as the impenitence of the named eighteenth-century infidels — Voltaire, Diderot, and their Prussian patron — at the close of their lives.

Litch quotes v.9 within his vv. 8-11 block (PREX1 179.1) but does not comment separately on the blasphemy-not-repentance reaction; he treats it as the natural response of the impenitent under unmixed wrath. Agrees with pioneer on the post-probation lock-in.

Andrews quotes v.9 within his block on vv. 8-11 (TMR 118.2) but offers no separate comment on the blasphemy. The closed-probation context (TMR 116.1: "wrath without mercy") governs the wicked's response — once Christ has stepped down from the heavenly sanctuary, character is fixed. Agrees with pioneer.

Haskell makes the Pharaoh-hardening parallel explicit and ties it to the prior rejection of mercy. "As long as probation lingered, God dealt with men in various ways to cause them to repent... When probation has closed, it will be seen that no power in heaven or in earth, could have turned the worldly-wise to the source of all true wisdom. 'Ephraim is joined to his idols: let him alone'" (SSP 281.1). On the blasphemy: "those without a shelter, who suffer from the intense heat... blaspheme God and repent not" (SSP 280, on vv. 8-9). Agrees with pioneer.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 32-33
modernReadings.html: Blasphemy-not-repentance is structurally Pharaoh-like; Bohr explicitly draws the Pharaoh parallel that Smith leaves implicit. Verbatim: "The reaction of the wicked to the plagues will reveal that their choice is irreversible. Instead of repenting, each plague further enrages the wicked against God's people like they did with Pharaoh in Egypt (Revelation 16:8-11)" (p. 33). On the closed-probation lock-in: he reads vv. 8-11 alongside v.11's "did not repent of their deeds" as evidence that "those who belong to the kingdom of the beast are beyond repentance because their decision is set in stone" (p. 37, on v.11 — same logic governs v.9).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at this verse)
notes.html: The Pharaoh-hardening parallel (Exod 8:32; 9:34-35), the appeal to closed-probation logic at Rev 15:8, and the Dan 7:25 cross-link as the verse's "blasphemy fulfilled in its terminal form" are structural extensions; Smith only notes the fact of the blasphemy here.

verses.text: And the fifth angel poured out his vial upon the seat of the beast; and his kingdom was full of darkness; and they gnawed their tongues for pain.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW collapses verses 10-11 into a single composite vision of the post-probation horror: "The inhabitants of earth had suffered the wrath of God in the seven last plagues. They had gnawed their tongues for pain and had cursed God. The false shepherds were signal objects of Jehovah’s wrath." She fixes the seat of the beast not on Bonaparte’s 1798 humiliation (Miller’s reading) but on the eschatological scene immediately preceding the voice of God: the false shepherds — the apostate ministers who refused the third angel’s message and led their flocks against the Sabbath-keeping remnant — become "signal objects" of judgment, with eyes "consumed away in their holes, and their tongues in their mouths, while they stood upon their feet" (echoing Zech 14:12). This is the eschatological-future reading characteristic of all the SDA pioneers: the fifth vial is still future and falls on Rome and its Protestant daughter-bodies in the closing crisis. The vial inaugurates the final chaos that climaxes in v.17.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 10-11 together in one quotation block at DAR 645.5; comment at DAR 646.1
pioneerReadings.html: **Plague poured on the papal seat at Rome.** Smith: "An illustration of this vial will be found in Exodus 10:21-23. It is poured upon the seat of the beast, the papacy. The seat of the beast is wherever the papal See is located, which has been thus far, and without doubt will continue to be, the city of Rome. 'His kingdom' probably embraces all those who are subjects of the pope in an ecclesiastical point of view, wherever they may be" (DAR 646.1). Smith also draws the chronology argument: "An important fact is established by this testimony; namely, that the plagues do not at once destroy all their victims; for some who were at first smitten with sores, we find still living under the fifth vial, and gnawing their tongues for pain" (DAR 646.1).

Miller reads the fifth vial as the French Revolution and Napoleonic dismantling of the Papal throne, c. 1798. "The seat of the beast must mean those ten kingdoms on which the woman sitteth, which is ancient Rome. Full of darkness must mean full of wickedness, confusion, and every evil work. Gnawed their tongues for pain shows shame, disgrace, and disappointment." He narrates the fulfillment with deliberate concreteness: "When Bonaparte began his extraordinary career, exalted to the pinnacle of power, he dethrones the pope (whose power and authority had made kings and emperors quail at his feet, who had ruled over the nations with despotic sway for more than 1200 years), and makes Rome the second city of France." Under this vial "the bastille was demolished, the inquisition destroyed, torture suppressed, and the power of the Papal clergy restrained." This is the same 1798 anchor Smith later uses for the fifth vial and converges directly with Litch (PREX1) and Andrews (TMR). All four pioneers stand on the same historicist hinge: the wounding of the beast under Napoleon.

Literal darkness paralleling the ninth Egyptian plague (Exod 10:21-23). Litch quotes the Exodus passage in full and then sums the cumulative horror: "What a terrible scene! with all their grievous sores, blood to drink — stagnant blood — and putrid fish filling the waters, scorched with burning heat; and then, to crown the whole, the whole kingdom of Anti-Christ is to be full of darkness. O, what a picture of woe! Reader, make haste to escape it" (PREX1 180, top). Litch identifies the kingdom of the beast = "the kingdom of Anti-Christ" = the papal-political domain. Agrees with pioneer on the papal-seat identification, anchored via Exod 10.

Andrews anchors the fifth plague in the Egyptian darkness of Exod 10:21-23: "And the Lord said unto Moses, Stretch out thine hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, even darkness which may be felt... but all the children of Israel had light in their dwellings" (TMR 119.1, quoting the OT). He then quotes Litch's summary verbatim: "On this subject, Litch remarks: 'What a terrible scene! with all their grievous sores, blood to drink — stagnant blood — and putrid fish filling the waters, scorched with burning heat; and then, to crown the whole, the kingdom of Anti-christ is to be full of darkness'" (TMR 119.2). Andrews thus carries forward Litch's papal-throne identification. Agrees with pioneer.

Literal darkness on the papal seat, with the Dark Day of 1780 as warning-type. Haskell: "The fifth vial was poured upon the seat of the beast. The developments in the last days, reveal the persecuting spirit of the beast and his image. All the world wondered after the beast, and looked to his man-made power in preference to the God of light and love. Thick darkness covered all the land of Egypt for three days, so that men could not leave their homes. This was a type of the darkness of the fifth plague" (SSP 281.3). On the typological warning: "Men scoffed when they heard that the darkening of the sun in 1780 was a token of the approaching day of God. Some of these men will be living, when the sun refuses to shine upon the whole earth. They blaspheme because of the heat of its rays; and then gnaw their tongues in anguish during the bitter night which settles upon the earth" (SSP 281.3). On Satanic exposure: "Satan, once light bearer in the heavenly court, claimed that light dwelt in him. This will be a time for him to manifest his power; but the world finds that its prince, with all his followers, is enshrouded in the same dense gloom. The light shines only upon the houses of Israel" (SSP 282.1). Agrees with pioneer.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 36-37
modernReadings.html: Plague of darkness on the papal See; Bohr identifies the beast = Rome papacy, the throne = Vatican City, the kingdom = the beast's global supporters. Verbatim: "The beast under the sixth [sic — context: the fifth] plague is the same as the beast of Revelation 13 and the little horn of Daniel 7 that spoke blasphemies against the Most High, persecuted the saints of the Most High, thought it could change times and law and ruled for 1260 years. The beast is a symbol of the Roman Catholic Papacy. The throne is the center of government from where the beast rules. In other words, this plague falls on the governing See of the beast. The papacy's center of power is in Vatican City within the confines of the ancient city of Rome" (p. 37). Bohr adds a Rev 13:3 / 13:7 / 17:15 cross-link to argue the "kingdom" = global supporters, "peoples, multitudes, nations, and tongues" (p. 37). Verbatim on the darkness: "The darkness that afflicts the throne of the beast and his kingdom is supernatural and global" (p. 37) — and a Zech 14:12-13 cross-link makes the wicked "kill one another with the weapons they intended on using to destroy God's people" (p. 37). Agrees with pioneer on the papal-seat identification.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Rule XI (literal-first) + Rule XII (the "seat of the beast" cross-references to Rev 13:2's papal seat).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at this verse)
notes.html: The Greek-_thronos_ / Rev 13:2 trace argument and the "man of sin sitting in the temple of God" cross-link are structural extensions; Smith's argument here is direct identification of the See of Rome and the Exod 10:21-23 parallel.

verses.text: And blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pains and their sores, and repented not of their deeds.

pioneerReadings.html: For EGW, v.11 is the impenitence-clause that seals the doctrine of post-probation hardening. "They had gnawed their tongues for pain and had cursed God" — even under the unmediated wrath of God the wicked do not repent because they cannot: Christ has ceased His intercession (GC 627.3-628.2). The plagues do not produce repentance; they only ratify the verdict already rendered when probation closed. EGW also notes the moral inversion: "After the saints were delivered by the voice of God, the rage of the wicked multitude was turned upon each other. The earth seemed to be deluged with blood, and dead bodies were from one end of the earth to the other." The fifth vial blasphemy is therefore not merely vocal defiance but the unmasking of the heart that had refused mercy under the third angel’s message. This converges with Miller, Smith, and Andrews on the Pharaoh-type hardening, but EGW alone develops the post-deliverance fratricide of the wicked as the immediate sequel.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 10-11 within the block at DAR 645.5; chronology comment at DAR 646.1
pioneerReadings.html: The sores from the first plague are still on the wicked under the fifth. Smith explicitly uses v. 11 to establish the cumulative chronology: "An important fact is established by this testimony; namely, that the plagues do not at once destroy all their victims; for some who were at first smitten with sores, we find still living under the fifth vial, and gnawing their tongues for pain" (DAR 646.1). Smith then challenges the past-fulfilment school by demanding historical evidence: "Can judgments so terrible be inflicted, and nobody know it? If not, where is the history of the fulfilment?… When did the subjects of the beast gnaw their tongues for pain, and at the same time blaspheme God on account of their sores?" (DAR 646.2).

Verse 11 is for Miller the impenitence-clause of the fifth vial: "Yet, after all this wonderful display of God’s judgments upon the beast and kingdoms of ancient Rome, they repented not, but, Pharaoh-like, they blasphemed God, because of their pains and their sores." Miller’s Pharaoh-typology is exegetically pointed — he treats the impenitence under the fifth vial as a deliberate echo of the Egyptian plague-cycle, where successive plagues hardened rather than softened Pharaoh. The implication is that the Papacy after 1798 follows the same script: humiliated, wounded, full of confusion and shame, yet still defiantly blasphemous. The verse anchors the doctrine that the vials produce hardening, not repentance, in the wicked — a structural point all the pioneers agree on and which carries directly into the final scene of v.21.

Litch reads v.11 inside his vials 4-5 block (PREX1 179) and again grounds the imagery on the Egyptian plague-cycle — citing Exodus 10:21-23 for the fifth vial’s "thick darkness" that "may be felt." His tableau is deliberately compounded: "What a terrible scene! with all their grievous sores, blood to drink, stagnant blood, and putrid fish filling the waters, scorched with burning heat; and then, to crown the whole, the whole kingdom of Anti-Christ is to be full of darkness." The impenitence of v.11 ("blasphemed the God of heaven… and repented not of their deeds") is for Litch the inevitable Pharaoh-pattern: as Egypt hardened under accumulating plagues, so the Antichristian kingdom hardens under accumulating vials. His pastoral payoff is direct: "Reader, make haste to escape it. Watch ye, and pray always, that ye may be accounted worthy to escape all these things." This reading is future-eschatological like Smith’s and EGW’s, not retrospectively political like Miller’s 1798 reading.

Andrews includes v.11 within his vv. 8-11 block (TMR 118.2) without separate treatment of the impenitence note; the wrath-without-mercy frame established at TMR 115-116 governs the wicked's response throughout. Agrees with pioneer.

Haskell roots impenitence in the same Pharaoh-typology: "As men did then, so will they do in the end of time; for men's hearts are the same in all generations. The repentance of Pharaoh was like that of Cain — it was sorrow for suffering, not sorrow for sin. This will be the same in the days of the last plagues" (SSP 278.1). And on the door-shut logic: "When probation has closed, it will be seen that no power in heaven or in earth, could have turned the worldly-wise to the source of all true wisdom. 'Ephraim is joined to his idols: let him alone'" (SSP 281.1). Agrees with pioneer on character-fixation after the close of probation.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 36-37
modernReadings.html: Bohr makes the character-fixation explicit at this verse. Verbatim: "The expression 'and they did not repent of their deeds' indicates that those who belong to the kingdom of the beast are beyond repentance because their decision is set in stone" (p. 37). On cumulation: he treats the plagues as "consecutive and cumulative" with the first four as "jeopardy plagues" and the last three as "deliverance plagues" (paraphrase of pp. 31-32), so the sores of v.2 are still present at v.11 — agreeing with Smith.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at this verse)
notes.html: The explicit "close of probation in Rev 15:8 was decisive — character cannot be re-cast after the door is shut" framing is a structural extension; Smith's point in DAR 646.1 is about plague cumulation, not character-fixation.

verses.text: And the sixth angel poured out his vial upon the great river Euphrates; and the water thereof was dried up, that the way of the kings of the east might be prepared.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW does not treat the Euphrates symbolism in DAR’s Ottoman frame; she reads v.12 eschatologically as the eve of the final deception. "Fearful sights of a supernatural character will soon be revealed in the heavens, in token of the power of miracle-working demons. The spirits of devils will go forth to the kings of the earth and to the whole world, to fasten them in deception, and urge them on to unite with Satan in his last struggle against the government of heaven." The drying of the Euphrates serves, in her framing, as the clearing of the way for the gathering of all earthly powers under Satan’s leadership — the same scene Smith narrates as the closing crisis. EGW’s emphasis is less on identifying "Euphrates" with a specific empire than on the supernatural deception that immediately follows: "Persons will arise pretending to be Christ Himself… They will perform wonderful miracles of healing and will profess to have revelations from heaven contradicting the testimony of the Scriptures." This converges with Smith and Andrews on the eschatological gathering but is silent on the question Litch and Smith dispute (literal river vs. Ottoman power).

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 12-16 together in one quotation block at DAR 646.3; sixth-plague exposition at DAR 647.1-652.6
pioneerReadings.html: **Euphrates = the Ottoman / Turkish empire; its drying up = the consumption of that power.** Smith canvasses the literal-river view, rejects it, and concludes: "These objections existing against considering it a literal river, it must be understood figuratively as symbolizing the power holding possession of the territory watered by that river, which is the Ottoman, or Turkish, empire" (DAR 648.2). The internal Revelation cross-reference fixes the symbol: "It is so used in other places in the Scriptures. (See Isaiah 8:7; Revelation 9:14.) In this latter text, all must concede that the Euphrates symbolizes the Turkish power; and being the first and only other occurrence of the word in the Revelation, it may well be considered as governing its use in this book" (DAR 648.3). Mechanism — withdrawal of European support: "By their sufferance alone that government now exists, and when they shall withdraw their support, and leave it to itself, as they will do under the sixth plague, that symbolic river will be wholly dried up; Turkey will be no more, and the way will be all open for the nations to make their last grand rally to the Holy Land" (DAR 648.6). The "kings of the east" = "the nationalities, powers, and kingdoms lying east of Palestine… The millions of Mohammedans of Persia, Afghanistan, Toorkistan, and India will rush to the field of conquest in behalf of their religion. (See more about Turkey in Daniel 11:40-45.)" (DAR 648.6). Smith notes the empire's present (in his day) decay as preliminary: "The event that takes place under the sixth vial is the entire and utter consumption of that power, not its preliminary state of decay, which is all that now appears" (DAR 649.1).

Miller’s reading of the sixth vial is the Ottoman-decline reading later codified by Smith. "The scene has now changed from Europe to Turkey. The river Euphrates means, in prophecy, the people of that country bordering on the river, and, of course, refers to the Turkish power, as I have formerly shown in my lecture on the fifth and sixth trumpet." "Water thereof was dried up" is "an emblem of the power and strength of that kingdom being diminished, or taken away." Miller enumerates the historical drying: Russian conquests in the late wars with the Turks; the revolt of Ali Pacha; the Greek War of Independence; subsequent revolts of Albanians and Georgians and other "distant parts of the empire becoming disaffected." His judgment in 1842: the Turkish Empire is now so wasted that "to compare her now with her former greatness, would be like comparing a fordable stream with the great river Euphrates." This is the same symbolic Ottoman reading Smith adopts in DAR and diverges sharply from Litch’s later literal-Euphrates reading in PREX1.

Literal Euphrates dried up, paralleling Cyrus' takeover of Babylon and the Red Sea / Jordan crossings. Litch reads the verse as straight-literal geography — and his Cyrus type is exactly the cross-reference the editor's note flags as the type Bohr vacates. Verbatim: "The great river Euphrates will be as literally dried up, to make a highway for the nations to come to that battle, as the Red Sea was for the Israelites to pass over dry-shod" (PREX1 180.1). And more explicitly on the Cyrus type: "'The great river Euphrates' will be as literally dried up to make way for the kings of the eastern world to come up to Jerusalem and Palestine to that battle, as the same river was dried up before Cyrus, when he entered and took the city of Babylon; or as the Red Sea and river Jordan were dried up to make a highway for Israel through their bed. The effects of the sixth vial will be, first, to dry up the waters of the river, to make a highway; and, secondly, to send forth the spirits of devils to deceive, by miracles, the kings of the whole earth, and their armies, and gather them" (PREX1 183.1). Litch differs from Smith on the Euphrates symbolism: Smith reads Euphrates as a symbol for the Ottoman / Turkish power (DAR 648.2-3, via Rev 9:14), while Litch reads it as a literal river to be literally dried up. Both reject Bohr's spiritualization, but for opposite reasons.

Andrews quotes vv. 12-16 in a single block and reads the sixth vial's twofold effect: "The effect of the sixth vial will be, first, to prepare the way for the kings of the East to come up to the great battle; and, secondly, to send forth the spirits of devils to deceive, by miracles, the kings of the whole earth, and their armies, and to gather them" (TMR 120.2). The drying of Euphrates is preparatory for the gathering; the gathering itself is described "very fully" in Jeremiah 25 (TMR 120.3-4). Andrews does not develop the Ottoman / Turkish identification at length — his focus is on the gathering of nations to Palestine for the battle, anchored in Joel 3:1-16 and Zephaniah 3:8 (TMR 121.1). Agrees with pioneer in reading the sixth vial as a literal preparation for the literal gathering of the kings.

Haskell adopts Smith's Ottoman-Turkish symbol-identification verbatim and extends it with the meeting-place geography: "But when the sixth plague is poured out, there is no restraining hand. The Turkish power designated as the River Euphrates, which has separated between the East and the West, gives way; and like the rushing together of mighty storm clouds, the armies of the earth, striving for the territory, meet in the valley of Jehoshaphat — the ancient meeting place for Egypt and Assyria, known in the Hebrew as Megiddo, and in Greek as Armageddon" (SSP 284.1). The valley of Jehoshaphat geography is anchored in 2 Kgs 23:29 (Pharaoh Necho passing through Megiddo to the Euphrates to fight Assyria at Carchemish). Agrees with pioneer's Ottoman-symbol identification.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 58, 62, 115
modernReadings.html: **The single most consequential spiritualization in Bohr's Revelation framework.** Bohr's full annotated rendering of the verse: "Then the sixth angel poured out his bowl on the great river Euphrates [Babylon's river], and its water [multitudes, nations, tongues and people] was dried up [withdraw their support from Babylon], so that the way of the kings from the east [Jesus and His angels — picked up in Revelation 19:11-21] might be prepared [before Jesus comes He must unmask the religious leaders]" (p. 62). On the symbolic identification of the river: "Ancient Babylon was seated upon the waters of the Great River Euphrates so the waters upon which the harlot sits are the waters of the Euphrates. However, this is not a literal harlot or a literal river. The harlot represents an apostate church and the waters represent multitudes, nations, tongues and people" (p. 58). On the meaning of the drying: "Revelation 16:12 portrays moment of the sixth plague after the close of probation when the persecuting waters (multitudes, nations, tongues and people) of Babylon withdraw their support and then drown her" (p. 62). On the kings-from-the-east identification: "The kings from the east — Jesus and His angels — come for the one-sided final battle against the kings of the earth, the beast and the false prophet" (p. 116). The OT type is the literal Cyrus-takes-Babylon (Dan 5; Jer 50-51; Isa 41, 44, 45) which Bohr renders as a "symbolic and global" antitype: "This entire scenario is applied symbolically and globally in Revelation 17" (p. 39).

items.body: Bohr applies Rev 17:15's gloss ("the waters where the harlot sits are peoples, multitudes, nations, and tongues") to Rev 16:12. But Rev 17:15's gloss is _scope-bounded_ — the angel says "the waters which thou sawest, _where the whore sitteth_." This is a specific identification for that specific vision, not a universal symbol-dictionary entry. Importing it backward 1 chapter requires extending the angel's gloss past the boundary the angel set.

The trace of "Euphrates" gives literal historical contexts: Gen 15:18 (Abraham's land boundary); 2 Kgs 23:29 (Pharaoh Necho's battle); **Jer 51:36, 63-64 (literal Euphrates dried up under Cyrus to take Babylon — a literal historical fulfillment Daniel himself witnessed and that Isaiah 44:27-28 predicted by name)**. The Bible's _consistent_ gloss on a "drying-up Euphrates" is the literal-historical Cyrus-takes-Babylon event. Pioneer reading: that historical event is the _type_ of which Rev 16:12 is the antitype — Ottoman Turkey dries up, the "kings of the east" (Persia, Afghanistan, India = the same eastern direction Cyrus came from) flow westward. Bohr's reading vacates the type.
The literal-political-Ottoman reading does no violence to nature and gives a precise geographic fulfillment.

Bohr's reading "kings of the east = Jesus and His angels" requires Rev 16:12 to depict the Lord's coming. But the Lord's coming in Revelation is described elsewhere (Rev 1:7; 19:11-16) without the _king-of-the-east_ designation — and the _Persian-kings-of-the-east_ anchor (Isa 41:2 calling Cyrus the "righteous man from the east" who comes to deliver God's people) is the natural OT type for kings-of-the-east deliverance, not a Christ-typology.

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Euphrates (Catalog A) — **the master spiritualization**; Kings of the East (Catalog A).

extensions.label: Extension beyond Smith (not in DAR)
extensions.html: The Cyrus-takes-Babylon type from Jer 51:36, 63-64 / Isa 44:27-28 as the load-bearing _type_ of which Rev 16:12 is the _antitype_ is the editor's structural reading; Smith's argument runs through Isa 8:7 + Rev 9:14, not the Cyrus type. The dating ledger (1840 / 1853-56 / 1877-78 / 1912-13 / 1914-18 / Allenby 1917) extends well past DAR's publication horizon — Smith witnessed only the early steps (1840 Edict of Toleration, Crimea, Russo-Turkish 1877-78); the WWI / Allenby completion is post-Smith documentation, not Smith's prose.

verses.text: And I saw three unclean spirits like frogs come out of the mouth of the dragon, and out of the mouth of the beast, and out of the mouth of the false prophet.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads the three unclean spirits as the climactic accomplishment of Satan’s deception, with Spiritualism as the masterpiece. "Satan has long been preparing for his final effort to deceive the world. The foundation of his work was laid by the assurance given to Eve in Eden: ‘Ye shall not surely die.’ … Little by little he has prepared the way for his masterpiece of deception in the development of spiritualism. He has not yet reached the full accomplishment of his designs; but it will be reached in the last remnant of time." She links v.13-14 directly to Spiritualism as the doctrinal infrastructure of the final delusion, which "the whole world will be swept into" except those kept by the word of God. Her identification of the three powers in the later sections of GC is: the dragon = Satan/Spiritualism, the beast = Roman Catholicism, the false prophet = apostate Protestantism. This converges with Smith’s DAR identification (paganism/papacy/Protestantism) on the second two terms but identifies the dragon with Spiritualism rather than pagan civil power — a notable shift toward the doctrinal triad.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 12-16 within the block at DAR 646.3; spirits comment at DAR 649.2-650.2
pioneerReadings.html: **Triple alliance = Paganism, Catholicism, Protestantism**, with Spiritualism as the principal agency. Smith: "The sources from which these spirits issue, denote that they will work among three great religious divisions of mankind, represented by the dragon, the beast, and the false prophet, or Paganism, Catholicism, and Protestantism" (DAR 650.2). On Spiritualism as the agent: "The agency now already abroad in the world, known as modern Spiritualism, is every way a fitting means to be employed in this work… before the spirits can have such absolute authority over the race as to gather them to battle against the King of kings and Lord of lords, they must first win their way among the nations of the earth, and cause their teaching to be received as of divine authority, and their word as law. This work they are now doing" (DAR 649.2).

Miller reads v.13 as deciphering "the signs of the times." "Three unclean spirits — by this we must understand three wicked principles. Frogs — I understand to show us that it is political. As frogs came over the land in the judgment on Egypt, and pervaded every house, even the palace of the king, so do politics. Mouth denotes orders or commands." Miller’s identification of the three powers is: the dragon = "the kings of the earth"; the beast = "Papacy"; the false prophet = "Mahometanism." All three "have the spirit of devils": the dragon is identified with the devil (Rev 12:9); the Papacy receives its power from the dragon and "shall go into perdition" (Rev 13:2; 17:11), so "must belong to Satan’s kingdom"; the false prophet "is to be cast alive into a lake of fire burning with brimstone" (Rev 19:20). "All these powers have pretended to work miracles, to establish their authority over the bodies and souls of men." This is structurally identical to Litch’s 1842 reading (kings + popery + Mahomedism) and diverges from Smith and EGW’s later paganism/papacy/Protestantism triad by retaining Mahometanism as the false prophet.

Litch reads the three unclean spirits with a triad sharply different from Smith’s. "The three unclean spirits like frogs go forth from the beast, popery; the dragon, Mahomedism, (for the Mahomedans now possess the imperial power of the east;) and the false prophet, infidelity." Note the assignment: dragon = Mahomedism (Litch reasons that the Mahomedans possessed imperial power in the east where John saw the dragon-symbol); beast = popery; false prophet = infidelity (modern atheistic/deistic systems). All three are "the spirits of devils, working miracles, or prodigies, like the magicians of Egypt, by which Pharaoh was deceived and fought against God and his hosts, even to the last. So the world of the ungodly will be deceived, and gathered against him that sitteth on the horse and his army, with the vain hope of triumph." This is a major divergence from Smith’s DAR triad (paganism/catholicism/protestantism) and from Miller’s (kings/papacy/Mahometanism). Litch’s 1842 reading places Mahomedism as the dragon — a position later abandoned by Adventism after Smith’s synthesis.

Andrews quotes vv.12-16 as a unit (TMR 120.1) and treats v.13 functionally rather than offering a unique triad identification. He emphasizes that "the effect of the sixth vial will be, first, to prepare the way for the kings of the East to come up to the great battle; and, secondly, to send forth the spirits of devils to deceive, by miracles, the kings of the whole earth, and their armies, and to gather them." The spirits of v.13 are therefore the active instrument of the gathering, not merely descriptive. Andrews then explicitly distinguishes preparation from fulfillment: "The present development of the spirits of devils in our land, we do not regard as anything but their preparatory work; for there is always a period in which Providence is preparing those agents which are to fulfill prophecy; but the fulfillment of prophecy begins at the point where the agents are prepared to act the predicted part" (TMR 121.2). This is a careful methodological move — affirming that Spiritualism’s rise in the 1850s is preparatory to v.13 without yet being its fulfillment. This converges with EGW (Spiritualism = ripening) and Smith.

Haskell adopts the Smith/EGW triad explicitly and adds an ecclesiastical-political dimension. "Paganism (the dragon), the papacy (the beast), and fallen Protestantism (the false prophet), join hands. Urged on by the unclean spirits, deadly decrees are issued by this threefold union, and Satan himself appears in person." Haskell describes Satan working "in a way never before known"; "the principles which made Rome the most oppressive government, are revived and strengthened. The miracle-working power of Spiritualism adds strength to the oppression." The three unclean spirits are therefore the doctrinal animators of a coalition of three falling religious systems that issues death-decrees against the commandment-keeping remnant. This converges fully with Smith and EGW and diverges sharply from Miller (kings/popery/Mahomet) and Litch (Mahomet/popery/infidelity). Haskell also incorporates the eschatological climax: "the angels loose the winds of strife; and marshaled by the great commander of the legions of darkness, the nations gather for the great battle of Armageddon."

Loughborough does not develop a separate triad-identification for the three unclean spirits, but he uses v.13 to clinch his argument against modern Spiritualism. The unclean spirits are not departed humans but "the spirits of devils, working miracles, which go forth unto the kings of the earth and of the whole world, to gather them to the battle of that great day of God Almighty." He couples Rev 16:13-14 with 1 Tim 4:1 ("in the latter times some shall depart from the faith, giving heed to seducing spirits, and doctrines of devils") and Rev 19:20 to identify modern spiritism as the present-day form of the demonic miracle-working power that the sixth vial unleashes. This is a polemical use of the verse against the rising spiritualist movement of the 1850s-70s, not a vial-historicist identification of the three powers, and it converges with EGW’s GC 561.2 framing of Spiritualism as the masterpiece of deception that ripens under the sixth vial.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 63, 65-66
modernReadings.html: Bohr's annotated rendering: "And I saw three unclean spirits [three evil angels] like frogs coming out of the mouth of the dragon [the secular powers of the world], out of the mouth of the beast [the papacy], and out of the mouth of the false prophet [apostate Protestantism]" (p. 63). On the timing: "However, verses 13 and 14 are parenthetical and take us back to the last remnant of probationary time" (p. 62). On the counterfeit-godhead structure: "The counterfeit godhead also has three entities and three unclean angels that proclaim their message and they will, for a time, be perfectly united" (p. 66) — mirroring the triune Godhead's three angels of Rev 14:6-12. Dragon identified through a Rev 12:4, Ezek 29:3 trace plus EGW (GC 438, 54): "while the dragon, primarily, represents Satan, it is, in a secondary sense, a symbol of pagan Rome" (p. 65, quoting EGW). Beast = papacy (Rev 13:1-10); false prophet = "apostate Protestantism in the United States" (p. 66).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **No violation.** Bohr's triple identification matches the pioneer's. The small wrinkle (dragon = secular powers / Satan-through-political-systems vs. dragon = paganism + Satan) is interpretive emphasis, not a rule break. Both readings track Rev 12, 13 cross-references correctly per Rule XII.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at this verse)
notes.html: The Exod 8:1-6 frog / Lev 11:10 uncleanness cross-references and the "mouth = doctrinal/verbal deception" gloss are structural extensions; Smith's argument runs on the three-religious-divisions identification + Spiritualism's preparatory work, not the frog-symbol etymology.

verses.text: For they are the spirits of devils, working miracles, which go forth unto the kings of the earth and of the whole world, to gather them to the battle of that great day of God Almighty.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW treats v.14 as the explicit identification of the agencies of final deception. "Fearful sights of a supernatural character will soon be revealed in the heavens, in token of the power of miracle-working demons. The spirits of devils will go forth to the kings of the earth and to the whole world, to fasten them in deception, and urge them on to unite with Satan in his last struggle against the government of heaven." She makes the gathering universal — "rulers and subjects will be alike deceived" — and grounds it in the supernatural counterfeit: persons claiming to be Christ, miracles of healing, revelations from heaven "contradicting the testimony of the Scriptures." For EGW, the only safeguard is the word of God: "Except those who are kept by the power of God, through faith in His word, the whole world will be swept into the ranks of this delusion" (GC 561.2). The verse therefore functions as the doctrinal hinge of the closing crisis — the moment when Spiritualism’s preparatory work becomes the active gathering of all nations to Armageddon.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 12-16 within the block at DAR 646.3; gathering comment at DAR 650.1
pioneerReadings.html: Demonic miracle-working spirits deceive the nations into the final battle. Smith: "To many it may seem incredible that the nations should be willing to engage in such an unequal warfare as to go up to battle against the Lord of Hosts; but it is one province of these spirits of devils to deceive, for they go forth working miracles, and thereby deceive the kings of the earth, that they should believe a lie" (DAR 650.1). On the locale of the gathering: "Where is the battle to be fought? — Near Jerusalem. (Joel and Zephaniah.) But Jerusalem is in the hands of the Turks; they hold possession of the land of Palestine and the sacred sepulchers. This is the bone of contention" (DAR 648.6).

For Miller, v.14 explains how the three unclean spirits gather the kings: by working miracles like the Egyptian magicians. Each of the three powers — kings, papacy, Mahometans — teaches a distinct false political-religious principle, and the verse identifies the gathering as "to the battle of the great day of God Almighty." Miller insists the gathering is universal: "the kings of the earth and of the whole world." He then telescopes immediately to v.15 ("Behold, I come as a thief. Blessed is he that watcheth and keepeth his garments, lest he walk naked, and they see his shame"), treating the warning as addressed to those "engaged in this political warfare, and in those popular and men-made societies of the day" (MWV2 213.1). The signal-content of v.14 for Miller is therefore that political fanaticism — under religious sponsorship — is the device by which Satan gathers humanity for its last self-destruction, with Egypt and its magicians as the controlling type. This converges with Litch on the Pharaoh/magicians typology and with Andrews on the universal scope.

Litch reads v.14 through a controlled exegetical analogy: as Pharaoh’s magicians deceived him into pursuing Israel into the Red Sea despite repeated divine warnings, so the spirits of devils will deceive the kings of the earth into pursuing Christ Himself into final battle. "So might Pharaoh have known that he could not conquer Israel, after all the manifestations of God’s power which he had witnessed; but yet he rushed madly on, with his eyes open, time after time, into ruin. Why did he do it? I answer, ‘the spirits of devils,’ in his magicians, deceived him."
