Litch then anchors the gathering on Old Testament prediction: Joel 3:1-2, 9-16 (gathering to the valley of Jehoshaphat); Zeph 3:8 ("my determination is to gather the nations, that I may assemble the kingdoms, to pour upon them mine indignation"); and Rev 19:19 (the kings of the earth and their armies gathered to make war against the rider on the white horse). The miracles of v.14 are therefore not generic wonders but the specific instrument by which all nations are deceived into the absurd act of warring against Christ in person.

Andrews develops the methodologically careful reading of v.14: the spirits of devils have a preparatory phase and a fulfillment phase. "The present development of the spirits of devils in our land, we do not regard as anything but their preparatory work; for there is always a period in which Providence is preparing those agents which are to fulfill prophecy; but the fulfillment of prophecy begins at the point where the agents are prepared to act the predicted part. Thus Rome, though it constituted the fourth empire, was founded when Babylon, the first empire, was in the height of its glory. But when Greece, the third empire, had fulfilled its part, Rome was prepared to act the part assigned it in prophecy. It is thus that we understand the work of the spirits. Under the sixth plague, the spirits will be prepared to act the part predicted in verse 13." Andrews thus refuses to identify mid-nineteenth-century Spiritualism with the fulfillment of v.14; it is preparation. Fulfillment awaits the actual outpouring of the sixth vial. This is an exegetical discipline that converges with EGW (Spiritualism not yet at its full accomplishment, GC 561.2) and is more cautious than Litch and Loughborough on the question of present-day fulfillment.

Haskell reads v.14 as the active production of the gathering itself. "Urged on by the unclean spirits, deadly decrees are issued by this threefold union, and Satan himself appears in person. The angels loose the winds of strife; and marshaled by the great commander of the legions of darkness, the nations gather for the great battle of Armageddon." The gathering is therefore not abstract: the spirits’ "miracles" function to (a) coerce the threefold union of paganism, papacy, and apostate Protestantism into deadly decrees against the saints, (b) release the restraining winds of strife (Rev 7:1), and (c) bring Satan personally to the field. Haskell notes the inversion: "Hitherto the hand of God has controlled in battle. His voice"… but in this final battle Satan, not God, marshals the armies. The verse therefore marks the complete unmasking of the cosmic conflict: the kings of earth, manipulated by demonic miracles, are arrayed against the Lamb. Converges with Andrews on universal scope and with EGW on Satan’s personal appearance.

Loughborough cites v.14 as decisive against the popular nineteenth-century claim that miracle-working proves a true prophet. "Certain spirits that will do a special work under the sixth of the seven last plagues, are called ‘the spirits of devils, working miracles, which go forth unto the kings of the earth and of the whole world, to gather them to the battle of that great day of God Almighty.’" His reasoning: if miracles prove truthfulness, then the false prophet of Rev 19:20 — which is explicitly said to have wrought miracles "with which he deceived them that had received the mark of the beast" — must be a true prophet, "and even Satan is a true prophet." The reductio is decisive: the same Scripture that predicts the miracles of v.14 also identifies them as satanic in origin and deceptive in effect. The exegetical payoff: in the last days, miracle-claims must be tested by the word of God, not the reverse — converging directly with EGW (GC 561.2) on the Scripture-only safeguard.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 63-64, 66
modernReadings.html: Demonic spirits work miracles to gather the wicked globally on Satan's side, mirroring the three angels of Rev 14:6-12 gathering the righteous. Verbatim annotated: "For they are spirits of demons, performing signs, which go out to the kings of the earth and of the whole world, to gather them to the battle [against God in the person of His witnesses] of that great day of God Almighty [for the battle of Armageddon]" (p. 63). On global vs. local gathering: "The purpose of the three counterfeit messages is to gather the entire world (Revelation 13:3) to follow and worship the beast" (p. 66). On the location of the gathering: Bohr's "spiritual" Jerusalem reading replaces Smith's Palestine geography — "in Revelation 14:14-20 the winepress outside the city is not merely in the valley of Jehoshaphat but in the whole world (Revelation 16:1). Therefore, Jerusalem where God's people are gathered must also be a worldwide Jerusalem" (p. 69).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation on demonic-deception identification. The spiritualizing of the battle's locale (the Jerusalem-is-global gloss) carries through to v.16 where it becomes a violation; here Bohr is still inside John's own language.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at this verse)
notes.html: 1 Tim 4:1 / 1 Cor 10:20-21 cross-references and the Joel 2:11, 31 / Zeph 1:14-18 anchor for "the great day of God Almighty" are structural extensions; Smith's cited supports are Joel and Zephaniah generally (DAR 648.6) without verse anchors.

verses.text: Behold, I come as a thief. Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments, lest he walk naked, and they see his shame.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW pairs v.15 with Matthew 24:44 as a single parenthetical warning embedded in the prophetic sequence — the thief-coming and the garment-keeping form one composite admonition to the church living through the closing scenes. "In such an hour as ye think not the Son of man cometh. Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments, lest he walk naked, and they see his shame." The garments are the righteousness of Christ, kept by daily watchfulness and obedience; the shame is the exposure of those who, like Belshazzar, have professed God’s name but lived in unrenewed apostasy. EGW reads the verse not as a postponed parenthesis but as an embedded warning whose force is felt precisely in the sixth-vial scene: it is in the moment of the universal deception of v.13-14 that the call to keep one’s garments becomes most acute. Converges with Smith and Andrews on the parenthesis-as-warning function and with Litch on the connection to the parable of the ten virgins (Matt 25).

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 12-16 within the block at DAR 646.3; v. 15 comment at DAR 650.3
pioneerReadings.html: Smith reads v. 15 as a _retroactive_ parenthesis, not a post-probation address. He raises the obvious objection — "Probation must have closed, and Christ have left his mediatorial position, before the plagues begin to fall. And is there danger of falling after that?" — and answers: "It will be noticed that this warning is spoken in connection with the working of the spirits. The inference therefore is, that it is retroactive, applying from the time these spirits begin to work to the close of probation; that by an interchange of tenses common to the Greek language, the present tense is put for the past; as if it had read, Blessed is he that hath watched and kept his garments, as the shame and nakedness of all who have not done this will at this time especially appear" (DAR 650.3).

Miller treats v.15 as the central pastoral warning attached to the gathering of v.13-14. "We are here notified that his coming will be like a thief to those who are engaged in this political warfare, and in those popular and men-made societies of the day; and we are told that those will be blessed who watch, that is, for his coming, and the signs of the times, and that keepeth his garments unspotted from these worldly institutions, which engender strife and animosity among brethren." The garments are kept clean specifically by separation from the political fanaticism of the day — the very societies and parties that the three unclean spirits inhabit. Miller’s exhortation is direct: "Be warned, dear Christian, ‘enter into thy chamber, and hide thyself for a little moment, until the indignation be over and past, that ye need not be ashamed before him at his coming’" (Isa 26:20). This converges with EGW on the watchfulness-and-garments theme but is more politically pointed: Miller treats the verse as a call to ecclesiastical-political withdrawal from corrupt societies, not merely a generic moral warning.

Litch reads v.15 as the explicit signal that the sixth vial is followed immediately by the eschatological gathering of Matthew 25 — the bridegroom and the ten virgins. "After the great river Euphrates is dried up and the mustering of the nations takes place, then let the people of God look up, for their redemption draws nigh. Let them keep their lamps trimmed and their light burning, and keep oil in their vessels, with their lamps. Then, when the proclamation is made, ‘Behold the bridegroom cometh, go ye out to meet him, they may open unto him immediately, and go in with him’ to the marriage, before the door is shut." Litch then identifies "The gathering of the nations before the Son of man, spoken of in the latter part of the 25th chapter of Matthew" with the moment of v.15-16 — "not that the general resurrection will then take place, but all nations which are alive at that time will be judged." The verse therefore signals the close of probation for the living: "the moral character of each individual must be decided, in order to his admission to the blessedness of the first resurrection, or to his destruction."

Andrews reads v.15 in tight chronological binding with v.12-14. "The coming of Christ as a thief (see Matthew 24:42-44) does not take place until after the sixth vial is poured out." This is an important exegetical commitment: the thief-coming language of v.15 is not a generic warning floating free of the vial sequence; it is specifically a sixth-vial signal. Andrews thus reads the verse as marking the chronological hinge between the gathering (v.12-14) and the consummation (v.17-21): Christ’s coming-as-a-thief follows the gathering of the nations and precedes the seventh vial’s "It is done." The implied corollary — affirmed throughout TMR — is that the saints who survive into the sixth-vial period must be ready, having received the seal of the living God before the plagues begin. This converges with EGW (COL 319) and Litch on the watchfulness theme but ties the timing more tightly to the vial schema than either.

Haskell reads v.15 as the closing diagnostic on those who will not survive the gathering. "The wicked, with hearts like adamant, see not the signs of His coming, but blaspheme, and to them He comes as a thief." The thief-coming language is therefore not principally for the saints (who, by SSP 287’s framing, have already heard the voice of God proclaiming the hour of the Saviour’s coming) but for the wicked — those still in business, in war-preparations, in plans to destroy the remnant when Christ’s arrival catches them. The garments-and-shame imagery cuts both ways: those who walk naked are those who, despite being warned through the third angel’s message and the closing plagues, never put on the imputed righteousness of Christ. Haskell’s emphasis is on the moral inversion: a peace surpassing understanding rests on the saints in the midst of the trouble, while the wicked are "blanched" with terror at the cloud in the east (SSP 288.1). The verse therefore parses the human race finally into two camps at the moment of the gathering.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 54, 64, 112
modernReadings.html: Bohr reaches the same parenthetical conclusion as Smith but with explicit ESV / verse-flow argument. Verbatim: "Revelation 16:15 is a parenthetical statement that encourages God's people, during the gathering time' to prepare for the close of probation and to gather on God's side in the final battle… This warning would be fruitless after the close of probation because by then all cases will have been decided. The ESV puts this verse in parentheses indicating that it breaks the flow of the previous verses. The context proves that the ESV is correct. You will notice that verse 14 ends with the word 'gather' and verse 16 picks up with the word 'gather'" (p. 64). Bohr makes a Rev 3:18-19 cross-link explicit (p. 55) — exactly the "garments = robe of righteousness" structural link Smith does not develop. On the litmus-test framing: "What will determine whose side we are on in this great final battle? Revelation 16:15, in the middle of the Armageddon message, has the answer" (p. 54). Bohr's parenthetical reading agrees with Smith's structural conclusion (the verse interrupts the gathering narrative) but reads the verse forward-looking (counsel for the gathering-time saints) rather than retrospective.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR)
notes.html: The Matt 24:43 / 1 Thess 5:2, 4 / 2 Pet 3:10 / Rev 3:3 cross-link for the "as a thief" formula and the Rev 3:18 / 19:8 cross-link for "garments = robe of righteousness" are structural extensions. The framing of the verse as "a warning for the sealed saints to watch through the eschatological crisis" _inverts_ Smith's plain reading — Smith treats it as a retrospective address to the _pre-probation_ unprepared, not as live counsel to post-probation saints.

verses.text: And he gathered them together into a place called in the Hebrew tongue Armageddon.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW treats v.16 as the culminating naming of the gathering-place but does not develop a separate Megiddo-Hebrew etymology (as Haskell does in SSP 284). The substance of her reading is contained in GC 624: the kings of the earth, deceived by miracle-working demons, are gathered "to unite with Satan in his last struggle against the government of heaven." The "place" is not principally geographical for EGW but moral and cosmic — the assembly-point of the wicked under Satan’s personal command for the climactic war against Christ and His people. She also writes in 1890: "Solemn events before us are yet to transpire. Trumpet after trumpet is to be sounded, vial after vial poured out one after another upon the inhabitants of the earth. Scenes of stupendous interest are right upon us." The naming of Armageddon is therefore a marker that the entire vial-cycle is converging on a single event — the personal coming of Christ to deliver His people and destroy the gathered hosts. Converges with all four pioneers on the gathering motif but is least committed to a specific geographical location.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 12-16 within the block at DAR 646.3; Armageddon-grammar comment at DAR 650.3-652.6
pioneerReadings.html: Smith resolves the singular-verb / plural-subject grammar puzzle, then identifies Armageddon as literal Megiddo. He defends the reading "the spirits gathered the kings together" against the rival "Christ gathered the saints to the New Jerusalem" reading: "by these authorities it is shown that the persons gathered are the minions of Satan, not saints; that it is the work of the spirits, not of Christ; and that the place of assemblage is not in the New Jerusalem at the marriage supper of the Lamb, but at Armageddon (or Mount Megiddo), 'at the battle of that great day of God Almighty'" (DAR 652.5). He then anchors the geography: "The hills of Megiddo, overlooking the plain of Esdraelon, was the place where Barak and Deborah destroyed Sisera's army, and where Josiah was routed by the Egyptian king Pharaoh-Necho" (DAR 652.6). Smith's biblical anchors are Barak/Deborah (Judg 4-5) and Josiah/Pharaoh-Necho (2 Kgs 23:29-30 / 2 Chr 35:22).

Miller has a striking and distinctive reading of v.16. He identifies "Armageddon" with its Hebrew etymology and applies it geographically — but not to Palestine. "‘And he gathered them together into a place called in the Hebrew tongue Armageddon’ (awake to the spoil)." Following the dragon-and-beast typology of Rev 12-13, Miller speculates that the last great battle of the dragon’s power "will be in America; and if so, it must be mainly in these United States" — because the language "and went… that the kings of the east might be prepared" indicates a westward direction. Miller anticipates that the political fanaticism of the three spirits will pervade "all the departments of life — the social, civil, and religious" and culminate in a great struggle on American soil. This is the most distinctively American reading of any of the pioneers, and it diverges from Litch’s literal-Jerusalem reading (PREX1 183) and from Haskell’s symbolic-Megiddo reading (SSP 284). Miller’s point is not that Armageddon is geographically America but that the principles symbolized by the gathering will reach their crisis where they will reach their crisis where political-religious fanaticism is most active.

Litch reads Armageddon literally and geographically. "The great river Euphrates will be as literally dried up to make way for the kings of the eastern world to come up to Jerusalem and Palestine to that battle, as the same river was dried up before Cyrus, when he entered and took the city of Babylon." The battle-site is therefore Palestine, and Litch links the scene to Ezekiel 38-39: "The same great battle is described in the 38th and 39th chapters of Ezekiel. Gog and Magog are all the devil’s armies." He further binds the scene to Zechariah 14 — "The Lord, and all his saints with him, is to come down on the Mount of Olives to fight the battle and drive out all the wicked from Jerusalem, and take possession of the place and fight the battle spoken of by Ezekiel 38 and 39." Litch’s reading is therefore literal-territorial: the nations are literally gathered to the land of Israel, with the Mount of Olives as the staging-ground for Christ’s personal intervention. This diverges sharply from Miller (America), Smith (symbolic), and Haskell (symbolic Megiddo as a global "place of the troops").

Andrews reads v.16 in continuity with Old Testament gathering-prophecies. The naming of Armageddon points the reader to Joel 3:1-2, 9-16 (gathering of the nations to the valley of Jehoshaphat), Zephaniah 3:8 (the determination "to gather the nations, that I may assemble the kingdoms"), and Jeremiah 25:30-33 (the Lord roaring from on high, with a controversy against all flesh, the slain "from one end of the earth even unto the other"). Andrews does not propose a single geographical site but treats Armageddon as the symbolic name for the universal gathering of the wicked to oppose Christ at His coming. He explicitly links it to Rev 19:19-21 (the kings of the earth and their armies gathered to make war against the rider on the white horse), so that "Armageddon" and the "battle of the great day" of Rev 19 refer to the same event. This converges with Haskell on the symbolic-universal reading and stands between Litch (literal Palestine) and Miller (literal America), refusing both literalizations.

Haskell offers the most exegetically developed Armageddon reading among the pioneers. "The Turkish power designated as the River Euphrates, which has separated between the East and the West, gives way; and like the rushing together of mighty storm clouds, the armies of the earth, striving for the territory, meet in the valley of Jehoshaphat, — the ancient meeting place for Egypt and Assyria, known in the Hebrew as Megiddo, and in Greek as Armageddon. The word itself means ‘the place of the troops’." He then anchors the typology in two biblical battles fought at Megiddo: (1) Deborah and Barak against Sisera, captain of Jabin king of the Canaanites, where God fought from heaven and the stars in their courses fought against Sisera (Judg 4-5); and (2) the death of Josiah by Pharaoh Necho, who was passing to "the stronghold of the Abyssinians on the Euphrates" (2 Kgs 23:29), commemorated in "the mourning of Hadadrimmon" (Zech 12:11). The verse therefore signals the last great contest between the nations under the sixth plague — the antitypical Megiddo where God will again fight from heaven for His people. Haskell also cross-references Dan 11:45 (the King of the North’s end at Jerusalem) as a parallel.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 113-114
modernReadings.html: **Spiritualized as "Mount of the Congregation."** Verbatim, with full Hebrew etymology argument: "The word Armageddon is a compound name that comes from two Hebrew words: Har (mount) and mogged (congregation). Putting the two words together, we find that the wicked are gathered for the final battle at the Mount of the Congregation. As is well known, the Hebrew language had no vowels. Thus the vowels 'a' in Har and 'o' and 'e' in mogged are not in the Hebrew text. This means that in the original Hebrew we find the consonants hr mggd. The Greek word Har-magedon is borrowed from the Hebrew and has the identical vowels as Har-mogged" (p. 113). On the double-gathering: "Both Daniel and Revelation, tell us that God will gather His people to spiritual Mt. Zion where they will congregate to worship the Lord… Revelation describes a two-fold gathering. God's people are gathered globally to spiritual Mt. Zion (the Mount of the Congregation) and the wicked are gathered globally around it at Armageddon in an attempt to destroy it" (p. 113). Bohr's anchor for _mo'ed_ / _mogged_ is Isa 14:13-14 (Lucifer's boast about "the mount of the congregation on the farthest sides of the north") + Ps 48:1-2 (Mt. Zion "on the sides of the north"): "God's dwelling place is on Mt. Zion on the sides of the north. At the beginning of the great controversy in heaven, Satan aspired to take over the heavenly Mount of the Congregation in the sides of the north, but was defeated. Therefore, at the end, Satan will attempt to conquer the earthly mount where God's people have spiritually gathered to worship the Lord" (p. 114). Conclusion: "In Revelation 16:16 the kings of the earth and the whole world are gathered in the place whose name means 'Mount of the Congregation'" (p. 114). Bohr also explicitly rejects literal-Palestine fulfilment: "It is erroneous to teach that these prophecies will find a literal fulfillment with literal Israel in a literal geographical location" (p. 182, on the broader Armageddon-language complex).

items.body: The Hebrew compound "_Har-Megiddo_" is a _proper noun place name_ — it appears in Judg 5:19, 2 Kgs 9:27, 23:29-30, 2 Chr 35:22, Zech 12:11 as a _literal geographic location_ (the hill country / plain of Megiddo in northern Israel). Every OT occurrence is literal-geographic. Bohr's re-pointing of the Hebrew to _Har-mogged_ (Mount of Congregation) is not the standard Hebrew for "congregation" (which is _qahal_ or _edah_); _mogged_ / _mo'ed_ means "appointed time / appointment / meeting place" — and Isaiah 14:13 uses _har mo'ed_ in a context that is Lucifer's _boast_ about wanting to ascend to God's heavenly mountain, not a generic earthly gathering point. Stretching Isa 14:13's _cosmic-rebellion_ phrase to gloss Rev 16:16's place-name violates Rule XII (the trace gives Megiddo, not Mo'ed).

Literal Megiddo works — it is one of the most-fought-over battle sites in the ancient world (Egyptian-Hittite, Egyptian-Israelite, Assyrian, Macedonian, Roman, Mamluk, Ottoman, British 1917 — Allenby's victory at "Megiddo" earned him the title "Viscount Allenby of Megiddo"). The geographic precision fits Rev 16:16's "called in the Hebrew tongue" — a _Hebrew place name_, not a Hebrew symbolic re-pointing.

The pioneer reading walks every word: "place" + "Hebrew tongue" + named geographic location. Bohr's re-pointing requires reading "Armageddon" as a _coded symbolic name_ John fabricated, which the text does not signal.

extensions.label: Extension beyond Smith (not in DAR)
extensions.html: The fuller battle-of-Megiddo ledger (2 Kgs 9:27 Ahaziah; Zech 12:11; the Egyptian, Hittite, Assyrian, Persian, Macedonian, Roman, Crusader, Mamluk, Ottoman, British 1917 — Viscount Allenby of Megiddo) and the explicit Rev 16:16 ↔ Dan 11:45 ↔ Zech 12-14 cross-tie are structural extensions; Smith's explicit biblical anchors here are Judg 4-5 (Barak/Deborah) and 2 Kgs 23:29-30 (Josiah/Pharaoh-Necho).

verses.text: And the seventh angel poured out his vial into the air; and there came a great voice out of the temple of heaven, from the throne, saying, It is done.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW gives "It is done" its most vivid eschatological exposition. "It is at midnight that God manifests His power for the deliverance of His people. The sun appears, shining in its strength. Signs and wonders follow in quick succession. The wicked look with terror and amazement upon the scene, while the righteous behold with solemn joy the tokens of their deliverance. Everything in nature seems turned out of its course. The streams cease to flow. Dark, heavy clouds come up and clash against each other. In the midst of the angry heavens is one clear space of indescribable glory, whence comes the voice of God like the sound of many waters, saying: ‘It is done.’" The voice from heaven simultaneously announces the closing of probation, the deliverance of the saints, and the immediate onset of the seventh-vial cataclysm. The cosmic disturbances — clouds clashing, streams ceasing, mountains shaking — are the visible accompaniment of the divine fiat. EGW alone among the pioneers gives the verse a sustained dramatic narrative, and her account becomes the touchstone for all subsequent SDA exposition.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 17-21 together in one quotation block at DAR 652.7; seventh-plague exposition at DAR 653.1-656.1
pioneerReadings.html: **The seventh vial poured into the air = universal scope.** Smith: "Some of the plagues are local in their application; but this one is poured out into the air. The air envelops the whole earth; it follows that this plague will envelop equally the habitable globe. It will be universal. The very air will be deadly" (DAR 653.1). On "It is done": "'And there came a great voice out of the temple of heaven from the throne, saying, It is done!' Thus all is finished. The cup of human guilt has been filled up. The last soul has availed itself of the plan of salvation. The books are closed. The number of the saved is completed. The final period is placed to this world's history. The vials of God's wrath are poured out upon a corrupt generation. The wicked have drunk them to the dregs, and sunk into the realm of death for a thousand years" (DAR 656.1).

Miller treats v.17 as the key interpretive moment of the entire chapter. In the lecture introduction he poses the explicit question: "Show what may be understood by ‘It is done.’" The answer is structural: when the seventh angel pours his vial into the air, the great voice from the throne announces that the entire vial sequence is complete. Miller emphasizes that the pouring "into the air" denotes a universal-atmospheric judgment, not a localized one — the air being the sphere occupied by the prince of the power of the air (Eph 2:2), so that the last vial directly assaults Satan’s own domain. The verse therefore marks (a) the termination of probation, (b) the consummation of all the preceding plagues, and (c) the immediate onset of the closing convulsion in v.18-21. Miller’s emphasis converges with Litch (PREX1 184.3) and Andrews (TMR 122.1) on the structural function of "It is done" as the divine declaration ending the vial-cycle and inaugurating the immediate cataclysm.

Litch reads v.17 with deliberate phrase-by-phrase analysis. "‘Into the air.’ Denoting an all-pervading and universal judgment. ‘Saying, It is done.’ It is the last vial, and under it all the judgments of God are to be consummated, — the wicked be swept from the earth, and the conflagration of the world take place. ‘There were voices.’ The voice of the Lord is to be heard in that day, declaring his wrath upon his enemies." He cross-references Joel 3:16 ("The Lord also shall roar out of Zion, and utter his voice from Jerusalem; and the heavens and the earth shall shake") and Jeremiah 25:30-31 ("The Lord shall roar from on high… he will plead with all flesh; he will give them that are wicked to the sword"). For Litch, the seventh vial is therefore the complete consummation: every preceding judgment is summed up and ratified in the divine "It is done," after which the world’s conflagration immediately follows. The verse marks the absolute end of probation and the inauguration of the destruction of all wicked living. Converges with Miller, Andrews, EGW on the structural function of "It is done."

Andrews places v.17 at the head of his block on vv.17-21 and develops the voice-from-the-temple typology through the Old Testament. "The voice from the temple may be illustrated by the following texts: ‘The Lord, also, shall roar out of Zion, and utter his voice from Jerusalem; and the heavens and the earth shall shake; but the Lord will be the hope of his people, and the strength of the children of Israel.’ Joel 3:16. ‘Therefore, prophesy thou against them all these words, and say unto them, The Lord shall roar from on high, and utter his voice from his holy habitation; he shall mightily roar upon his habitation; he shall give a shout as they that tread the grapes, against all the inhabitants of the earth.’ Jeremiah 25:30, 31." The "It is done" is therefore the prophetically-predicted roar of Jehovah from His sanctuary, marking the close of the entire prophetic cycle. Andrews reads the verse as a covenantal moment: simultaneously the consummation of judgment against the wicked and the moment of refuge for the saints ("the Lord will be the hope of his people").

Haskell reads v.17 as the moment when the cosmic elements themselves rebel against the wicked. "While the nations are gathering for this great contest, the seventh angel pours out his vial in the air. The elements, which had heretofore mingled in giving life to man, clash together; and above the tumult, the mighty peals of thunder, and the flashes of lightning, the voice of Jehovah Himself is heard saying, ‘It is done.’" He immediately cross-references Isa 34:4-9 (the host of heaven dissolved, the heavens rolled together as a scroll, "the day of the Lord’s vengeance, and the year of recompences for the controversy of Zion"), Nahum 1:1-6 (the Lord’s way "in the whirlwind and in the storm"), and Micah 1:3-4 (the mountains molten under Him). For Haskell the verse marks both the close of probation and the inception of the visible second coming: "After the Father’s voice is heard saying, ‘It is done,’ His throne moves" (SSP 287.2). The voice of God simultaneously seals the saints, raises a special resurrection of those who died under the third angel’s message and of "they who pierced Him" (Dan 12:2; Rev 1:7), and announces the imminent personal appearance of Christ. Converges fully with EGW.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 32, 41, 54, 119
modernReadings.html: God's voice from the heavenly temple announces consummation; "it is done" is the moment the seventh plague is launched. Verbatim quoting EGW's GC 635-636: "In the midst of the angry heavens is one clear space of indescribable glory, whence comes the voice of God like the sound of many waters, saying: 'It is done.' Revelation 16:17" (p. 32, repeated p. 54). On the structural placement: "the central themes of the seventh plague are God's voice saying 'it is done' followed by an earthquake, thunder, lightning, terrific precipitation and the disappearance of mountain ranges and islands" (p. 41). Bohr ties v.17's "It is done" to the close of the gospel commission and the deliverance of God's people from end-time Babylon. Agrees with pioneer on universal consummation.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR)
notes.html: The Eph 2:2 "prince of the power of the air" cross-link and the John 19:30 / Rev 16:17 / Rev 21:6 "three terminal It-is-done pronouncements bracketing redemption history" structure are structural extensions; Smith's argument is universal-scope and consummation-of-the-saved-count, not Eph 2:2 / John 19:30 typology.

verses.text: And there were voices, and thunders, and lightnings; and there was a great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth, so mighty an earthquake, and so great.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads v.18 as the immediate cosmic accompaniment of "It is done." "Everything in nature seems turned out of its course. The streams cease to flow. Dark, heavy clouds come up and clash against each other. In the midst of the angry heavens is one clear space of indescribable glory… Graves are opened, and ‘many of them that sleep in the dust of the earth … awake, some to everlasting life, and some to shame and everlasting contempt.’ Daniel 12:2. All who have died in the faith of the third angel’s message come forth from the tomb glorified, to hear God’s covenant of peace with those who have kept His law." The "great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth" is therefore simultaneously: (a) the cosmic shaking that accompanies the voice of God; (b) the agency by which the graves are opened for the special resurrection of Dan 12:2 — both the faithful third-angel’s-message dead and the crucifiers (Rev 1:7); and (c) the inauguration of the seventh-vial cataclysm. EGW alone develops the special-resurrection dimension explicitly, drawing Dan 12:2 into the earthquake of v.18.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 17-21 within the block at DAR 652.7; voices/earthquake comment at DAR 653.3
pioneerReadings.html: Above the voices, thunders, lightnings stands the voice of God himself. Smith: "Above all will be heard the voice of God. 'The Lord also shall roar out of Zion, and utter his voice from Jerusalem, and the heavens and the earth shall shake; but the Lord will be the hope of his people, and the strength of the children of Israel.' Joel 3:16. (See also Jeremiah 25:30; Hebrews 12:26.) This will cause the great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth" (DAR 653.3).

Miller reads v.18 as the literal global earthquake that ends terrestrial history. He treats the voices, thunders, lightnings, and earthquake as the divine accompaniment of the conflagration that closes the day of the Lord. In his summary of all six previous vials as already fulfilled (MWV2 229.2), he writes pointedly: "Six of these plagues have accomplished in as literal a manner as we could expect, after a fair and scriptural explanation of the figures and metaphors used. And again I ask, Do you believe? You think, perhaps, you will wait until you see the hail come, and then you will believe." The earthquake of v.18 is therefore the next-in-sequence literal event for Miller, immediately following the close of the sixth vial (which he saw as already opened in his own day, c. 1822 onward). The verse functions as the unmistakable announcement of the second coming: a global, never-before-seen seismic event whose scope can only be matched by the consummation of all history. This converges with Litch on the literal-global earthquake and with Andrews on the climactic-eschatological reading.

Litch reads v.18 as the literal universal-scale earthquake whose magnitude exceeds all historical comparison. "‘And thunders, and lightnings.’ Exodus 9:23: ‘And Moses stretched forth his rod toward heaven: and the Lord sent thunder and hail, and the fire ran along upon the ground.’ As truly as the thunders and lightnings of Jehovah came on Egypt, and destroyed the Egyptians and the produce of the field, so truly it will come on the ungodly world, when God fills up the cup of his fury." On the earthquake itself: "The terrors of an earthquake are more easily experienced than described… But O, when it shall not desolate a single city only, but desolate the globe!!!" Litch cites the historical earthquake of Aleppo (PREX1 153) as a faint type and Ezek 38:19-20 as the prophetic prediction: "in that day there shall be a great shaking in the land of Israel; so that the fishes of the sea, and the fowls of the heaven, and the beasts of the field… shall shake at my presence; and the mountains shall be thrown down, and the steep places shall fall, and every wall shall fall to the ground." The verse therefore signals the global earthquake that fulfills Hag 2:6 — "I will shake not the earth only, but also heaven."

Andrews illustrates v.18 by stacking biblical earthquake-prophecies that match its scale. "The great earthquake may also be found spoken of in other scriptures: ‘The earth is utterly broken down, the earth is clean dissolved, the earth is moved exceedingly. The earth shall reel to and fro like a drunkard, and shall be removed like a cottage; and the transgression thereof shall be heavy upon it; and it shall fall, and not rise again.’ Isaiah 24:19, 20. ‘And the heaven departed as a scroll when it is rolled together; and every mountain and island were moved out of their places.’ Revelation 6:14; 11:19." Andrews therefore reads the seventh-vial earthquake as the consummation of the long-prophesied shaking of the earth — a single event of which Isaiah 24, Revelation 6:14, and Revelation 11:19 are convergent witnesses. The verse fulfills the Old Testament prophecies of cosmic dissolution at the day of the Lord and inaugurates the immediate physical end of the present world-order, opening directly into v.19-21’s scenes of Babylon’s fall, the islands fleeing, and the hailstorm.

Haskell reads v.18 as the moment when the visible response of nature ratifies the divine "It is done." "‘A great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth,’ shakes the earth to its very foundation. ‘And every island fled away, and the mountains were not found.’" He then contrasts the two simultaneous responses to the shaking: the wicked, "with hearts like adamant, see not the signs of His coming, but blaspheme, and to them He comes as a thief"; while "songs of deliverance" rise from the little companies of the saints, who echo Ps 46:1-3 — "God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble. Therefore will not we fear, though the earth be removed, and the mountains be carried into the midst of the sea." Haskell also folds in Heb 12:26-27 (the final shaking that removes "those things that can be shaken"), and like EGW (GC 637.1) he notes that "as the voice of God echoes through the earth, the earth trembles; the graves open, and those who have fallen asleep under the sealing message, come forth glorified" (SSP 287.2). Converges with EGW on the special-resurrection link.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 41, 54
modernReadings.html: Voices, thunders, lightnings and the great earthquake are literal cosmic upheaval at the seventh plague; God's voice causes the earthquake. Verbatim, quoting EGW's GC 636: "That voice shakes the heavens and the earth. There is a mighty earthquake, 'such as was not since men were upon the earth, so mighty an earthquake, and so great' Verses 17, 18. The firmament appears to open and shut. The glory from the throne of God seems flashing through. The mountains shake like a reed in the wind" (p. 54). Bohr's reading is essentially the GC-636 paragraph applied as exegesis. Agrees with pioneer.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR)
notes.html: The explicit "Lisbon 1755 = Rev 6:12 sixth-seal sign, not this final convulsion" disambiguation and the Hag 2:6-7 cross-link are structural extensions; Smith's cited supports here are Joel 3:16, Jer 25:30, Heb 12:26.

verses.text: And the great city was divided into three parts, and the cities of the nations fell: and great Babylon came in remembrance before God, to give unto her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads the dividing of "the great city" into three parts and the remembrance of Babylon as the moment Babylon receives the full cup of God’s wrath. The threefold division corresponds to the three component powers of v.13 — paganism (the dragon), the papacy (the beast), and apostate Protestantism (the false prophet) — and the cup of wine refers back to Rev 14:9-10 (the wine of God’s wrath threatened against the worshipers of the beast). For EGW the seventh-vial cataclysm is the moment when Babylon’s long-deferred reckoning is paid in full: the cities of the nations collapse together as the earthquake levels the structures of human apostasy. The verse therefore closes the cycle opened in Rev 14:8 ("Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city") — what was announced doctrinally by the second angel’s message is now executed physically by the seventh angel. Converges with Smith, Andrews, and Haskell on the threefold identification and with Bates (BP2 65.3) on the Babylon/three-parts ecclesiology.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 17-21 within the block at DAR 652.7; "great city" comment at DAR 653.4
pioneerReadings.html: Smith reads "the great city" as the _threefold composite of false religion_. "The great city is divided into three parts; that is, the three grand divisions of the false and apostate religion of the world (the great city), Paganism, Catholicism, and relapsed Protestantism, seem to be set apart each to receive its appropriate doom. The cities of the nations fall; universal desolation spreads over the earth; every island flees away, and the mountains are not found; and great Babylon comes in remembrance before God. Read her judgments, as more fully described in chapter 18" (DAR 653.4).

Miller reads "the great city" of v.19 as Rome, in continuity with his identification of the seat of the beast in v.10 as the ancient Roman ten kingdoms. Under the seventh vial, the great city is divided into three parts — Miller treats this as a structural disintegration of the Roman power, with the "cities of the nations" (i.e., the other capitals of the kingdoms that gave their power to the beast) collapsing simultaneously. "Great Babylon came in remembrance before God, to give unto her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath" — for Miller this is the lex talionis answer to the persecutions of vials two and three, now applied not to local European kingdoms but to the entire apostate world-system culminating in the Roman polity. The verse is the final reckoning of all that the second angel of Rev 14:8 had proclaimed: Babylon, having now reached the full measure of her iniquity, drinks the wine of wrath without mercy. Converges with Litch, Andrews, Haskell, Smith on the cup-of-wrath logic and the structural fall of Babylon.

Litch reads v.19 with deliberate emphasis on the unimaginable scale of the destruction. "For ‘the great city,’ Rome, ‘was divided into three parts, and the CITIES OF THE NATIONS FELL.’ They were laid in a heap of ruins. Think of ten thousand human beings buried in the ruins of the earthquake of St. Domingo last spring! But what is that to the time when all the cities of the earth are destroyed at a stroke, by the power of Omnipotence; when ‘every island shall flee away and the mountains are not found!’" Litch identifies "the great city" as Rome (in continuity with the sixth-vial geographical framing) and reads the three-part division as Rome’s ruin. He cross-references Ezek 38:19-20 (the day when "the mountains shall be thrown down, and the steep places shall fall, and every wall shall fall to the ground"). The verse therefore signals the simultaneous destruction of all human urban civilization under the seventh vial, with the typological St. Domingo earthquake of 1842 standing as a faint shadow of the global desolation.

Andrews quotes v.19 within his block on the seventh vial (TMR 122.1) and treats it as the structural disintegration of Babylon in fulfillment of Rev 14:8-10. The cup of the wine of the fierceness of God’s wrath that Babylon now drinks is the same wrath Rev 14:9-10 had threatened against the worshippers of the beast — proving the inner coherence of the Apocalypse’s judgment-cycles. Andrews does not develop the three-part division at length but treats it as the corporate identification of the threefold apostate religious power (paganism/papacy/apostate Protestantism, in continuity with v.13). For Andrews the verse is the moment Babylon’s cup, long filling, is finally drunk to the dregs: the announcement of Rev 14:8 ("Babylon is fallen") is now executed in v.19, just as the warning of Rev 14:9-10 is executed in v.21. The seventh vial is therefore the structural answer to the three angels’ messages. Converges with Smith, Haskell, and EGW on the cup-of-wrath logic and with Miller and Litch on the corporate scope of the destruction.

Haskell makes the threefold division of Babylon the central interpretive point of v.19. "Babylon, the nation of the earth which has long made nations drunk with the wine of her fornication, comes in remembrance before God in her threefold nature, as paganism, papacy, and apostate Protestantism, and is made to drink of the wine of the wrath of God." The three parts of the great city therefore correspond exactly to the three unclean spirits of v.13 — the same triad that orchestrated the gathering now suffers the gathered wrath. Haskell anchors the moment in his account of the saints’ deliverance: while Babylon drinks the cup of wrath, "those who are spiritually one with Him, are caught up to meet the Lord in the air, ‘And so shall we ever be with the Lord.’" The verse therefore marks the simultaneous destruction of Babylon and the rapture of the saints — the two-sided consummation of the great controversy. Converges with EGW on the threefold identification and with Bates (BP2 65.3) on the Babylon-as-organized-Churches ecclesiology, though Bates names the three differently (Roman/Greek/Protestant).

Bates uses v.19 to ground a distinctive ecclesiology of Babylon. "Mystery Babylon, the antitype of literal Babylon, which signifies confusion and mixture, represents the organized Churches of all descriptions, divided into three parts, Revelation 16:19, viz: Roman, Greek, and Protestant." Note the triad: Roman, Greek, and Protestant Churches — not paganism/papacy/Protestantism (Smith/Haskell/EGW) and not kings/papacy/Mahometanism (Miller). Bates is making an ecclesiological identification: the three "parts" of Babylon are the three great Christian communions, each fallen, each implicated in the apostasy from which God’s people must come out (Rev 18:4). The verse therefore grounds the "come out of her, my people" call: God’s remnant is summoned out of all three Christian bodies that together constitute Babylon. This is a significant divergence from the later Smith/Haskell/EGW consensus, which retains Protestantism as one of the three but substitutes paganism for Greek Orthodoxy. Bates’ reading reflects the 1847 Sabbatarian-Adventist polemic against the established Christian denominations as the targets of the second angel’s message.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 41, 67
modernReadings.html: Bohr's annotated rendering: "Now the great city [Babylon] was divided into three parts [three parts are in Revelation 16:13] before this the three parts had been united], and the cities of the nations fell. And great Babylon was remembered [reckoning day has come] before God, to give her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of His wrath [same words in the third angel's message and Revelation 19:15]" (p. 41). On the threefold division, Bohr explicitly cross-links back to v.13's dragon/beast/false-prophet — the same triad Smith identifies as Paganism/Catholicism/Protestantism but glossed through the Rev 13 / Rev 17 framework rather than Smith's historic three-religious-divisions catalog. On the cup-of-wrath chain: Bohr makes the Rev 14:10 cross-link explicit (p. 67) — exactly the structural extension Smith leaves implicit.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR)
notes.html: The framing of "great city = Babylon the great (Rev 17-18)" (with the divisions = dragon/beast/false-prophet of v.13) is an editor extension; Smith identifies the _threefold division_ directly with Paganism / Catholicism / relapsed Protestantism (the same triad he uses for v. 13), and treats Babylon as the broader apostate-religion whole that is now judged. The Jer 25:15-17 / Rev 14:10 cup-of-wrath cross-link is a structural extension not in DAR 653.4.

verses.text: And every island fled away, and the mountains were not found.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads v.20 as the literal geographical convulsion accompanying the voice of God. In her visions she describes the moment when the islands flee away and the mountains are not found as a physical reordering of the planet under the seventh vial — mountain chains sinking, vast lakes lifted into the sky, the surface of the earth made an immense plain in preparation for the final conflagration and the eventual new earth. Cross-references in her writings link the verse to Isa 24:18-20 (the earth reeling like a drunkard), Ps 46:1-3 ("the mountains carried into the midst of the sea"), and Heb 12:26-27 (the final shaking that removes "those things that can be shaken"). The verse therefore is not metaphorical for EGW but the actual physical reshaping of the globe, removing every refuge from the wicked and demonstrating the totality of the judgment. This converges fully with Litch (PREX1 185), Andrews (TMR 123), Miller, and Haskell on the literal-cosmic reading of the verse.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 17-21 within the block at DAR 652.7; comment at DAR 653.4-654.1
pioneerReadings.html: Universal desolation: "every island flees away, and the mountains are not found" (DAR 653.4). Smith uses the verse to underline that the wicked have no shelter from the hail: "But mankind, at this time, will have no shelter. The cities have fallen in a mighty earthquake, the islands have fled away, and the mountains are not found" (DAR 654.1).

Miller reads v.20 as the literal geographical end of the present world-order. The disappearance of islands and the leveling of mountains is for him part of the literal global earthquake that ends the day of the Lord — it is one of the unmistakable signs (alongside the hail of v.21) by which any honest reader will know that the chapter is fulfilled. Miller’s expectation was that vials 1-6 had already poured out in identifiable historical events by his lifetime (concluding with the Ottoman decline of the sixth vial, c. 1822-40), and that vials 7 — including the islands and mountains fleeing — was the next literal event on the prophetic calendar. His pastoral force: "You think, perhaps, you will wait until you see the hail come, and then you will believe" (MWV2 229.2) — but by then it will be too late. The verse therefore functions for Miller as a final empirical sign: when men see the mountains literally moving, they will know the chapter has reached its end. Converges with Litch, Andrews, EGW, and Haskell on the literal-cosmic reading.

Litch reads v.20 as the literal physical end of all human refuge. "After the cities fall, the islands flee away, the mountains, walls, fences and steep places, are all thrown down, and no place of refuge is found; then the overflowing hail-storm follows on the naked heads of a guilty race." The sequence is exact and chronological: first the cities collapse (v.19), then the islands and mountains disappear (v.20), then the hail strikes the now-exposed multitude (v.21). Litch reads each clause literally and cross-references Ezek 38:19-20 ("the mountains shall be thrown down, and the steep places shall fall, and every wall shall fall to the ground") and Isa 28:17 ("the hail shall sweep away your refuge of lies"). The verse therefore marks the moment when every conceivable shelter from the divine judgment is removed — a complete stripping of the wicked of every place to hide before the final hailstorm. Converges with Miller, Andrews, EGW, Haskell on literal-cosmic destruction.

Andrews quotes v.20 within his earthquake-block (TMR 123) and cross-references Rev 6:14 ("the heaven departed as a scroll when it is rolled together; and every mountain and island were moved out of their places") and Isa 24:19-20 ("the earth is utterly broken down, the earth is clean dissolved, the earth is moved exceedingly… and it shall fall, and not rise again"). For Andrews the verse is therefore the literal fulfillment of the sixth-seal cosmic dissolution that was anticipated in Rev 6:14 but waited for the seventh vial’s "It is done" to be executed. The structural inner-Apocalypse coherence is important to him: Rev 6:14, Rev 11:19, and Rev 16:18-20 all describe the same final convulsion, viewed from different angles in the visions. The disappearance of islands and mountains is part of the prophesied final shaking that removes "those things that can be shaken" (Heb 12:26-27), preparing the way for the new heaven and new earth that follow in Rev 21. Converges with the unanimous literal-cosmic reading of all the pioneers.

Haskell reads v.20 as part of the same convulsion that produces v.18-19 and as the immediate setting for the songs of deliverance among the saints. "‘A great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth,’ shakes the earth to its very foundation. ‘And every island fled away, and the mountains were not found.’ When the wicked have no shelter, then are heard songs of deliverance from the little companies. ‘God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble. Therefore will not we fear, though the earth be removed, and the mountains be carried into the midst of the sea.’" Haskell makes a sharp moral inversion the interpretive key: at the very moment the wicked lose every refuge, the saints sing Ps 46. The verse therefore parses humanity into two camps at the moment of the seventh vial: those whose refuge was always physical (mountains, cities, walls, hiding-places) are exposed, while those whose refuge was always God Himself find Him present. Converges with EGW on the protection of the saints during the final shaking and with Litch on the literal-physical removal of all refuge.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, p. 41
modernReadings.html: Bohr's annotated rendering: "Then every island fled away, and the mountains were not found [predicted at the end of the sixth seal in Revelation 6:14-16 and also in Revelation 20:11]" (p. 41). Bohr explicitly makes the Rev 6:14-16 cross-link that Smith leaves implicit, and adds the Rev 20:11 link to the post-millennial earth's passing away. He also quotes the EGW GC-636 description: "Mountain chains are sinking. Inhabited islands disappear. The seaports that have become like Sodom for wickedness are swallowed up by the angry waters" (p. 54).

notes.label: Note (not in DAR)
notes.html: Cross-links to Rev 6:14, 2 Pet 3:10, Isa 24:19-20, and the "preparing for the new earth" gloss are structural extensions; Smith treats v. 20 as part of the seventh-plague desolation, not as a re-leveling for the new earth.

verses.text: And there fell upon men a great hail out of heaven, every stone about the weight of a talent: and men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail; for the plague thereof was exceeding great.

pioneerReadings.html: EGW reads v.21 in tight binding with Isa 30:29-30: "Ye shall have a song, as in the night when a holy solemnity is kept; and gladness of heart, as when one goeth … to come into the mountain of the Lord, to the Mighty One of Israel. And the Lord shall cause His glorious voice to be heard, and shall show the lighting down of His arm, with the indignation of His anger, and with the flame of a devouring fire, with scattering, and tempest, and hailstones." The hailstorm is therefore the agency by which God scatters the armed multitudes who had gathered (under sixth-vial deception) in every quarter to destroy the saints. The talent-weight hail accomplishes two simultaneous works: (a) the literal destruction of the armed companies preparing to strike the remnant in one night, and (b) the fulfillment of Job 38:22-23 ("treasures of the hail… reserved against the time of trouble, against the day of battle and war"). The blasphemy of the wicked is for EGW the inevitable post-probation hardening — they cannot now repent, because Christ has ceased His intercession (GC 627.3, 628.2). Converges with all pioneers on the literal-eschatological hail.

pioneerReadings.citation: DAR covers vv. 17-21 within the block at DAR 652.7; hail comment at DAR 653.5-655.3
pioneerReadings.html: **Literal hail, stones of about a talent weight each.** Smith gives his weight reckoning: "'Every stone about the weight of a talent.' A talent, according to various authorities, as a weight, is about fifty-seven pounds avoirdupois. What could withstand the force of stones of such an enormous weight falling from heaven? But mankind, at this time, will have no shelter" (DAR 654.1). Smith's cited biblical supports are Isa 28:17 ("the hail shall sweep away the refuge of lies"), Isa 30:30, and Job 38:22-23 ("the treasures of the hail, which he has 'reserved against the time of trouble, against the day of battle and war'") (DAR 653.5). Smith devotes most of his exposition (DAR 654.2-655.3) to Commodore Porter's eyewitness account of a Bosporus hailstorm in _Letters from Constantinople_ as a "faint idea" of the scene, closing: "if such were the desolating effects of a hailstorm of ice, which discharged stones the size of a man's fist, weighing at most a pound or so, who can depict the consequences of that coming storm in which 'every stone' shall be of the weight of a talent?" (DAR 655.3).

Miller treats the talent-weight hail as the unmistakable empirical climax of the chapter. "Six of these plagues have accomplished in as literal a manner as we could expect, after a fair and scriptural explanation of the figures and metaphors used. And again I ask, Do you believe? You think, perhaps, you will wait until you see the hail come, and then you will believe." Miller’s pastoral force is direct: the hail of v.21 will be too late as a sign — it is the consummating act, not a warning. His reading is straightforwardly literal: hailstones each "about the weight of a talent" falling from heaven on a generation that, having refused every preceding mercy, will blaspheme rather than repent. This converges with Litch on the literal hailstones (citing Watson’s Bible Dictionary for the 125-lb talent weight, PREX1 187.1), with Andrews on the Egyptian-plague typology (Exod 9:22-25), and with EGW on the post-probation hardening. Miller’s distinctive contribution is the pastoral urgency: this is the last appeal, beyond which there is no recovery.

Litch is the most quantitatively precise of the pioneers on the hail. "Richard Watson, in his Bible Dictionary, says a talent is a hundred and twenty-five pounds. Others say ninety-six pounds, and some sixty pounds. Whichever it may be, nothing could stand before such a shower. A hailstone falling from heaven, would produce all the effect of a lead or iron ball of the same weight. What could endure a shower of cannon balls falling from heaven, each one weighing one hundred and twenty-five, or even sixty pounds?" He cites Fisk’s Travels in Europe on the 1835 Padua hail-storm (stones "as large as cannon balls" that "broke off the fingers, arms, noses, etc., of this extensive company of statues") as a faint shadow of what is coming. He also cross-references Exod 9:22-25 (Egyptian hail), Isa 28:17 ("the hail shall sweep away your refuge of lies"), and Job 38:22-23 ("treasures of the hail… reserved against the day of battle and war"). The pastoral force: "Reader, have you found a refuge from such a storm? The only place of refuge is the name of the Lord of hosts." Converges with all the pioneers on the literal-eschatological reading; uniquely quantitative in scope.

Andrews illustrates the hail of v.21 with a stack of biblical witnesses to its scope and severity. "And the Lord sent thunder and hail, and the fire ran along upon the ground; and the Lord rained hail upon the land of Egypt. So there was hail, and fire mingled with the hail, very grievous, such as there was none like it in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation." Exodus 9:22-25. "Hast thou entered into the treasures of the snow, or hast thou seen the treasures of the hail, which I have reserved against the time of trouble, against the day of battle and war?" Job 38:22, 23. "Judgment, also, will I lay to the line, and righteousness to the plummet; and the hail shall sweep away the refuge of lies, and the waters shall overflow the hiding-place." Isaiah 28:17. The verse therefore fulfills the prophesied "treasures of hail" reserved by God for the day of battle, executing the lex-talionis judgment on those who built "refuges of lies." Andrews concludes the chapter with the call: "May God count us worthy to escape the things coming on the earth, and to stand before the Son of man." Converges with Litch on the Egyptian-plague type and with EGW on the Isa 30 and Job 38 cross-references.

Haskell reads v.21 as the climactic display of post-probation hardening. "And yet with it all, when ‘there fell upon men great hail out of heaven, every stone about the weight of a talent,’ men still ‘blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail.’ The wicked, with hearts like adamant, see not the signs of His coming, but blaspheme, and to them He comes as a thief." The verse therefore caps the seventh-vial sequence with the final, irreversible verdict on the wicked: even under the literal hailstorm — described by Haskell with deliberate weight, "every stone about the weight of a talent" — the response is not repentance but blasphemy. He pairs the moment with Isa 30:29-30 (the saints’ song "as in the night when a holy solemnity is kept" while God lets fly "with scattering, and tempest, and hailstones"). The same hail therefore serves opposite ends: destruction for the wicked, deliverance for the saints. The chapter closes with the small cloud in the east (SSP 288.1) signaling the personal return of Christ. Converges with all the pioneers on the literal-eschatological reading and with EGW on the simultaneous-deliverance dimension.

modernReadings.citation: _Close of Probation to the New Earth_, pp. 41, 54
modernReadings.html: Literal hailstones of talent-weight; consummating destruction of Babylon. Bohr quotes the verse and EGW's GC-636 close together: "And great hail from heaven fell upon men, each hailstone about the weight of a talent. Men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail, since that plague was exceedingly great" (p. 41). EGW echo: "Babylon the great has come in remembrance before God, 'to give unto her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of His wrath.' Great hailstones, every one 'about the weight of a talent,' are doing their work of destruction" (p. 54, quoting GC 636). Bohr does not gloss the talent weight in pounds; he treats the hail as literal but supplies no avoirdupois figure (so the ~57 lb pioneer reckoning is a Smith-specific detail not echoed by Bohr). Agrees with pioneer on literality.

notes.label: Note (not in DAR at this verse)
notes.html: Cross-links to Exod 9:18-26, Josh 10:11, and Ezek 38:22 are structural extensions; Smith's explicit biblical anchors here are Isa 28:17, Isa 30:30, Job 38:22-23. Smith's talent reckoning is "about fifty-seven pounds avoirdupois" (DAR 654.1), not "75-100 pounds" — that range should be corrected to match Smith.

summaryHtml:
**Rev 16 violation summary:** Of 21 verses, Bohr breaks Miller rules on **2** (vv. 12 and 16). But these two are _the most load-bearing verses in pioneer Adventist end-time prophecy_ — Rev 16:12 is the Eastern Question hinge that links Daniel 11:40-45 to the Ottoman dissolution, and Rev 16:16 is the literal-Palestine geographic anchor of the final battle. The disproportionate weight of the two violations cancels out the chapter's otherwise rule-compliant exegesis.
